### Post by steveneddy on 2008-02-03
I am 43 years old. I have been using Ubuntu since 4.10. I used to try and break the system, and I was good at doing that, and I also learned a lot.

I use my System76 laptop, which came preinstalled with Ubuntu, for work mainly. I can access our UNIX system and work on our company system, even remotely if necessary, and I use the internet to search for other vendors where I may be able to purchase parts and pieces. 

I email, GIMP, maintain my web server remotely, watch movies, at work we use Streamtuner to tune music of all types, we pay bills online, use Pidgen to IM friends and family. After all that, at the end of the night, when the family is quiet and the neighbors are asleep, I try and take over the world.

Bwa-ha ha ha ha haaaaaa!

### Post by mmazzera on 2008-02-03
Im 25 and i started using Ubunttu maybe 4 months ago.. i switched to Ubuntu because i got tired of Microsoft and too take a look at that unknown world (for me) called Linux.
Of course at first i created a dual-boot with Windows XP (Note: im installed it at work) becuase i wasnt sure if i could make it work so i can do my daily job with it. I'va had the dual boot for about a month... then i realized that i was ready to make the full switch, so i did ....

It all started as a learning process and it changed the way i work, of course i do use Windows on a VirtualBox (10000000 times faster than a real PC) to run some Windows applications... i know... i could use WINE, but, there are only two things that i use on VB... 

1- Netsupport Manager (remote control application), there's a Linux Version but it happens to mess things up, even though that ITS the only application that claims to be Linux compatible :S

2- a CRM software that was entirely built on MSAccess database connecting to Oracle (completely stupid, i know, but thats the only thing we have right now....)

I recently was told about Virtual Box seamless mode, it blew me away... its f******* fantastic, the only thing is that i can have compiz enabled with seamless mode, but thats ok, still runs faster than Windows

Soooo, that's my short but growing experience with Linux.

Max Mazzera.

### Post by galeg on 2008-02-04
I am 58, and live in Melbourne Australia (recently retired from Senior Project Manager role in the ID division of large Australian bank)..
Three weeks ago built Ubuntu on a dual boot environment with XP as the other OS.
Hopefully will convert to total Ubuntu when I am able to source XP replacement applications or manage to get Wine to run them.
The biggest issues I have at the moment are DVD Decrypt / DVD Shrink, and BitTorrent running under Wine (have had a couple of goes to date without success, when time permits will try again). Have also experienced problems with Canon Scanner D1250U2 as no drivers appear to exist.
Currently use Ubuntu for Internet browsing, email, Open Office applications, and watching documentaries.

### Post by macogw on 2008-02-12
I'm 19.  I use it for everything.  I use it for IMing, web browsing, IRC, typing papers in LaTeX, programming (I'm a comp sci student), learning more about Linux*, looking stuff up when I don't understand the professors' lectures, reading articles about technology (and a lot of women-in-tech ones) and from the [ACM](http://acm.org), blogging, taking SkillSoft online courses (free online courses that ACM student members can take...it's how I study for the LPIC), ripping CDs and putting the oggs on my [iAudio](http://www.cowonglobal.com/product_wide/product_i7_feature.php), ssh-ing into servers to do web design, and GIMPing pictures.


* As I said, I'm a comp sci student.  I'm taking a Unix System Administration class, and it's *really* easy for me because I use Linux.  I keep being told not to answer all of the questions, to give the other students a chance.  The professor doesn't mind if I help the other students with their labs when I finish, since he's being asked for help by just about everyone in the room.

### Post by RandomJoe on 2008-02-16
I am 36.  I haven't had a Microsoft OS installed on any of my computers since somewhere around 1998.  Linux all the way, except for the Mac Mini which managed to survive a short time with OS X before it too went the way of the Penguin... :)

Wish I could say I've been totally Windows-free, but alas work requires Windows...  I do enjoy telling people "I don't do Windows" though! 

Besides the basic computer uses, I have a few nifty things I've set up here:
[LIST]
[*]1TB RAID for all my movies and music - who wants to mess with the discs all the time?
[*]My primary system is a quad-head.  Can have three flat panels (usually run two) plus a fourth head that connects to the projector in the living room (11 foot screen on the wall).
[*]Use the X10-style modules and a "firecracker" module on a serial port to turn lights on/off automatically.  Sure helps waking up in the morning when it's still dark.
[*]Used to have MythTV on a server in back so I could watch TV from any computer in the house - even the laptop while sitting outside.  Gave up on TV a while back though.
[*]I'm a ham radio operator - so one system runs a variety of ham applications, most often gMFSK to do PSK31 on HF.
[/LIST]

after upgrading my videocard to a 128 mb one: enjoying the true beauty of my monitor. 1280x1024!

i still use xp for posting on an msn group, playing a game and chatting via mirc.

everything else will be done under unbuntu from now on, which will take up most of the time.

list of activities: chatting on amsn, surfing, a game, websites, writing stuff, p2p, listening to music, viewing movies, the gimp, plus prolly a few more things.

my main reason for using ubuntu is that it will bring back the real fun in computing and will make my computer really mine!

### Post by dgoodma on 2008-03-05
58

I use Ubuntu, Linux Mint, and gOS to help prove in some things in a large corporate environment. Three or four years ago I looked at Linux , Fedora and CentOS, to see if they could replace Windows desktops. Things were not quite there then.

Things are getting closer now, but still not quite there. Two major things then were, lack of easy integration into Active Directory (now see [http://www.likewisesoftware.com](http://www.likewisesoftware.com) and using Evolution to Exchange. Both of those have really improved, and I have been able to demo them working in our environment.

In helping prove in things, I can "play" with Linux and try to increase my knowledge in doing so, "thinking" keeps the mind active and help ward of the effects of advancing age, or so they say :)

Really want one of the new small laptops... waiting for generation two, so some of the issues will have been worked out.

### Post by soro2005 on 2008-03-06
I'm 38, and I use Ubuntu for all purposes. When I occasionally boot into the XP that's also still somewhere around for the one thing I can't do with Linux (Sony Hi-MD) or to check out some web design in Safari and IEs, it makes me nervous to hear the fan spin because of poor process management and everything scrambling to haul in security updates. I find it interesting to think about how much money Bill Gates has taken out of the circuits of economy by inventing the concept of the CPU that's too busy for interaction and forcing people to sit idle while their computers finish whatever it is that they have to finish. But interesting, really, only because it is a case study in how completely arbitrary phenomena can come to be taken as natural givens (here the idea that your computer is unresponsive a couple of times a day/week). But these thoughts creep up only while I sit and wait for XP to come back, or whenever I speak to the question why and what for I use Linux.

### Post by yeats on 2008-03-15
I'm 34, and I am using Ubuntu for any purpose I can think of!  I use Firefox & Thunderbird, I did some video editing with Cinelerra, I'm a musician and have downloaded Ardour (multitrack recorder) and Hydrogen (drum machine) and plan to experiment with a low-budget studio situation.  I'm basically exploring Linux as a true replacement for Windows.  I'm a public librarian and would love to see more GNU/Linux usage, as nearly all of my colleagues are dissatisfied with Windows/MS Office & proprietary LIS software.

I'm a Windows XP user who has just gotten curious about what Linux is and how it all works.  I'm blogging my experiences at:

http://sharplinux.blogspot.com[/URL]

Feel free to take a look.

Chris

### Post by ellis rowell on 2008-03-15
I am 79 and have been using computers over 20 years.
I use Ubuntu for Office work (Open Office), website maintenance, emails, photo processing. I don't see the point of dual booting (if you crash the system you could hve problems with both systems), I prefer to go another way. I have installed a Super Rack (ViPower) so that I can close down the system I am using, swap the System HDD and fire up in the other system. I use a 250GB HDD (slave) for storing data which prevents the C: drive getting clogged up, and is available in both systems. I would get rid of Windows XP if I could only get my printers and scanners working  with Linux.

### Post by MattSMiddleton on 2008-03-22

### Post by drawmada on 2008-03-22
I'm almost 35 ... Started using ubuntu on Friday, March 21, 2008 because I received 2 CDs in the mail which I had ordered.  I installed it because my Outlook Express and Office started acting up. My two daughters, who are 6 and almost 8 respectively, are pleased as punch with it. They surf the net ... go play flash games, and so far haven't had too many problems compared to Windows!  I don't hear awwwwwww or Daddy! anymore lol ... I use ubuntu mainly for banking, basic Word documents, surfing, email.  I will explore even more as I am very pleased so far with the results!  Hats off to the whole crew who designed this easy to use OS! :)

### Post by sav2005 on 2008-03-23

LaTeX/TeX document preparation system
Quanta HTML/PHP editor
Dia diagrams and flow charts

KDissert mind mapping exports to LaTeX
mc - the best file manager since XTree Gold for DOS

mutt - sucks less than the others

Why I can't lose Windows: 
Dreamweaver, Flash, Illustrator, Photoshop are core teaching and learning tools for Multimedia students at our college.

### Post by Sean4000 on 2008-04-04
I'll write for my family here:

I'm Brett Adam Steele. I'm 5 years old and I use xubuntu 7.10 x32 for going to nickjr.com, noggin.com, and playhousedisney.com.  My brother, Sean4000, really hates the garbage that kids sites dump on Windows so he taught me how to use Linux!

I'm Cecile, Sean4000's mom. I'm 46 and I use xubuntu 7.10 x32 for checking email, online ordering, and instant messaging. With security through the roof, online ordering has become "safe" again.

I'm Justin, and I'm Whitney; ages 20 and 17 respectively. We use Apple. lol. Sorry. :(

I'm Mark, Cecile's husband, aged 47.  I use Vista because millions of others use it so it must be good. I did not convert my office to Apple or Linux but rather am planning to upgrade to Vista because they are going to "work all of the bugs out" someday.  I know Sean told me that Office and Quickbooks are for Mac but I rather the Windows version because they're more stable. I spend thousands of dollars on tech support.  My new tech guy, John, is great; he told me that Mac and Linux are loaded with viruses too and that because they're so small that no one would fix them in time. Go Microsoft!

P.S.: John left our family business' main financial computer without antivirus software for 5 weeks. 

All ofthe things I jokingly wrote in Mark's paragraph are true.

### Post by mmillman on 2008-04-11
> **ExpatPaul said:**
> Maybe Canonical should make more of the fact that, with Ubuntu, you can visit dodgy websites without having to worry about spyware...  ;)

Wow... didn't even think about that... it's even better for porn surfing =P

23 years old, just started using it.  Using it primarily for surfing, multimedia, overall stability/reliability, and pure aesthetics.  Plus, I enjoy the challenge of troubleshooting issues, and I like the terminal commands... it reminds me of a throw-back to the old days of MS-DOS.  I am very comfortable using command prompts... I simply need to learn all of the commands (or at least the most important/frequently used ones).   I haven't used dos in well over a decade and I still have a plethora of knowledge about all the different useful commands etc...  I hope to become that proficient with Linux next!

I've been using computers since I was 4 (my grandfather is a huge computer geek, and he always had/has the newest cool stuff...), I've owned several OLD systems (including an 80x88 and an 80x86 ), I've been building systems since I was about 12 give or take, and I can honestly say that I have not been more enthusiastic or excited about anything in the world of computers in my entire life as I am about Ubuntu!

### Post by RetiredInMaine on 2008-05-08

### Post by grashdur on 2008-05-10
I'm 40 and I use Ubuntu for word processing, spreadsheets, web browsing, web mail, watching YouTube, sometimes listening to music, occasionally editing music files. I love the crisp, stable, simple way that Ubuntu works compared to Windows. I also appreciate the lack of troubles caused by viruses and spyware, as well as the even greater problems caused by anti-malware programs in Windows.

My other computer is Win XP only. The main things keeping me from switching totally to Ubuntu: * No decent calendar program (I tried Evolution but it did nothing well except crash)  * No decent timer/stopwatch program (the "Stopwatch" program is pretty rudimentary and doesn't make a notification sound  * When a new OS version comes out, suddenly some programs and features stop working  * XSane scanner doesn't work with the latest versions of Ubuntu, and never did OCR  * I would also like some easy way to set up DVD playing for Ubuntu (I had it set up on a previous install, but now can't remember how I did it, and following instructions leads me only to error messages)

### Post by km6xz on 2008-05-11
58 going on 25. New to Ubuntu and have not had any problem at all with 8.04 on my ACER laptop Travelmate 4233.  Boots and runs faster then Vista Ultimate, and runs cooler, connects faster to my wireless access point and overall is a pleasure to use.
What do I do with it?  A bit of everything, web browsing, email, bittorent, word processing, spreadsheets and a little web development in php/mysql.  I got into Open Software through working with Apache, php and MySql and the whole philosophy is very exciting, it feels good in many ways. From that beginning I decided to explore more and ended up with Ubuntu and some good desktop applications. I still want to explore music production on this system, multitrack recording is something I did professionally for 25 years in the Studer/Neve/SSL era of analog recording. 
I had a chance to save the day for my girlfriend this weekend.  Her windows XP machine was getting to the splash screen and then a warning popped up indicating a virus was in control and laughed at us through the speaker before rebooting. I went home and got my ISO image DVD of Ubuntu and in 15 minutes had her machine working great...but different.  She only uses it for internet so she hardly notices the difference however since she was using FireFox before anyway. She is about as anti-tech as a person could be, and will never care about which OS is used as long as she can get to the internet. This case undermines the sayings about Linux being only for geeks. The coolest part of the new OS according to her is the start up bar of music.
Stan
St Petersburg Russia/San Francisco

67
My first introduction to computing was a course in Fortran IV on an IBM 360 mainframe. Then I used MS-DOS for several years (I believe I started with 2.0 and ended up around 6 or so). I used to have a one floppy version of Windows 1.some - useless but for a game or two. I have used Windows 3.1, Windows for Workgroups 3.11, NT 3.5, NT 4.0, Windows 95, Windows 98, XP, UBUNTU Gutsy, and now Hardy. I try to use Hardy for everything EXCEPT Photographs, scanning, e-mail and genealogy. Once I am sure of not losing my mail files, that will be moved over, but I see no need to be too hasty. (I am an Ent at heart).

### Post by BoDwayne on 2008-05-20
I'm 52 and I use Ubuntu for everything I used to use Windows for - browse internet, email, word processing, spreadsheet, presentations (all on Open Office), download and play movies, burn CD's - both data and pics, etc.  

I started out a year or two ago weaning myself of all Microsoft and mainstream applications, switching to Firefox, Thunderbird, Open Office, Foxit - all while still on Windows.  Then about 6 or 7 months ago I finally installed Ubuntu.  I was lucky enough to have a second computer at the time, so for a few weeks I had one running Ubuntu and one running Windows.  I was able to share files from the Ubuntu computer, reading, copying, editing and deleting files from and onto the Windows computer, but Windows was too self-involved to recognize Ubuntu and therefore had no idea the Ubuntu computer was interfacing with it and changing its files.  Sort of a one-way door.  What kind of security is that, Mr. MS?  Of course, I had to know the password to the Windows network to do so, but still. . . .  Anyway, after a month or two the Windows computer starting having problems and finally locked up (hmm . . . has that ever happened before?) and I set it aside and have never missed it since.  I'm happy to say that now I spend more time working with my computer than working on it.

Thank you, Ubuntu, Canonical, and the entire Linux and open source community!!

*I got tired of fixing the Windows, so I just took down the walls.*

---

### Post by gphaze on 2008-06-04
I'm in my *very* late 30s. I am an artist.

I am very happy with Mac OS X, but I am also very unhappy with the commercial software model, which is basically "our way only," and "use our (expensive) stuff or you're out in the cold!"

Plus, there's lots of brand-new software you can buy which basically ships full of bugs and the software companies' reply is "wait 'til the next version" which always costs still MORE dough, and comes with still MORE bugs,  but then I don't have to tell you all about those headaches...

I have always heard great things about Linux, and I like several titles which will run on a Mac but in X11. I'd like to use those great titles in a native environment, like Gimp, Inkscape, Scribus, and any NLE video 'ware out there...  and other x-platform titles but which can be used in Linux, such as Blender 3D.

pretty encouraged by the helpful community in linux land and looking forward to learning and using this great OS.

gphz

### Post by ellis rowell on 2008-06-07
I'm 79, started in computing in 1986 with a main frame, was seconded to another job where I realised that a small simple computer would help with my work. I bought my own (a Sinclair QL) joined the Ql Users ANd Tinkerers Association (QUANTA) and got to understand programming with Super Basic. In October 1988 I started QMAS (Quanta Mid-Anglia Subgroup) it is still in existence as Mid-Anglia Computer User Meetings. I used the QL, Amiga, various PC's. Our members used QL's, Apricot's, Apple Mac's and Acorn's. The most versatile machine in my opinion, was the Amiga, it's the only one that I know of, that you could put in the disk parameters and it could read and write a disk, no matter what system it was, it could also copy any disk with "bit copy" to make an exact replica.

I tried Red Hat v5.2 but couldn't get on with it, much later I started with Ubuntu (v6.04) then all the updates to v8.04, it gets better while Windows seems to get worse.

### Post by elashish on 2008-06-09

### Post by tucurious on 2008-06-11
I'm 59.  I use Ubuntu to work towards complete freedom from Microsoft. Tax return
was my last need for Micro OS.  Never understimate the useful role Microsoft
played in getting most of us online.  But I don't like the direction it is going
or the way it is doing it.
The cooperative nature of open source has it's own appeal. The hands on nature
of Linux gives me a feeling of having some control, a feeling rapidly going away
with Windows.
E-mail and web searches take up most  of my time. Spread sheets and to do lists
are common uses to organize my day.
I hope people will find some more respect for the folks who brought us this OS.
When the bugs are the size of turtles they are easier to find!

### Post by Murrquan on 2008-06-17
I'm 25, and I use Fedora. I just hang out here on the Ubuntu forums. ^.^

I'm a writer, so I use BasKet to manage my notes, while I produce copy in gedit. Amarok provides a soundtrack, and I use Firefox / Thunderbird for web access and email. I also play a few games, including some of my older Windows games through Wine, and use gThumb / F-Spot to manage my digital photos.

As a platform to work with, I really love Linux compared to Windows XP. It just feels a lot more obedient and responsive, and it doesn't keep tossing distractions at me to break me out of flow. Plus it's more secure, and it has nice desktop effects. Aesthetic concerns are important for us creative types!

I've had to learn a few things to get it to work, but I think that it's been worth it. Still, I'm considering buying a Mac for my next machine.

### Post by bkortleven on 2008-06-25
I'm a 30 year old Linux user (been using since RedHat 5.0), and am using Ubuntu for 99% of my Linux machines now (One box running a derivate of Debian for my tv server)...
I have several machines running at home: 
one P4-3GHz Dual Core machine for Storage and VMWare (holds 8 disks, total of around 2TB and runs 3-4 virtual servers including 2 Ubuntu machines)
one AMD64 X2 machine for 'Virtual Desktop' (again a VMWare setup with an ubuntu server as host system, and an ubuntu 8.04 desktop in it, connecting through NX and XDMCP from a thin client on my desk)
one Dell laptop ('old' inspiron 4000 p3 mobile machine) for surfing and playing around + tv frontend
one AppleTV running MythBuntu 8.04 (check my website to find out how I did it ;) )
one company laptop with Ubuntu 8.04 on it (we use Ubuntu for our desktops at work too)
one old P3 1GHz with Ubuntu 7.04 on it, runs in the datacenter, and hosts my website etc... Haven't had to restart it after the first 2 weeks of use... so it has been running since about 2-3 weeks after 7.04 came out...


That's about it...
My HTC Universal (PDA-phone) was running an Ubuntu derivate for ARM cpu's, but I messed up on the machine... Don't know if that counts as an Ubuntu PC too, as it's a mini-micro-laptop cell phone, full keyboard, vga screen, wifi and bluetooth, but no intel/amd/sparc cpu so not able to run the 'real' Ubuntu stuff...

### Post by OttifantSir on 2008-07-09
I'm 27 and I am completely lost. I KNOW it's possible, but as of yet, no luck in doing the following: Setting up a file-/media-/mail-server with three 1TB(986GiB) disks acting as one (trying to understand LVM) and serving everything mediarelated to a media-PC with no fuss after the initial setup. And keeping a record of all my mail and sending it to all my other computers (two more at the moment, three very soon)

Besides my wants, I have one computer for nothing else besides e-mail, e-shopping and office (old P3). A laptop which is used mainly for testing things and gets broken alot.
I am a media-oriented user of Ubuntu, but haven't completely forgotten what I liked in Windows: VLC and OpenOffice. However, Ubuntu has given me more insight into what makes computers work in the last two years than I ever learned in the (apx) 18 years prior, when using Windows.

Someday I WILL prevail over my own ignorance and make my setup work just as I want it to: Without fuss, without gripe, simple enough that my own grandmother wouldn't have any trouble understanding how to do things.

I am hoping for this: Server tucked away, running without notice, a position on the couch viewing HDTV through a PC, or playing a game, a computer that gets turned on when I need to do some work that can save all work to the server, and a laptop that can connect to all of this the moment it's turned on (connected to a network), even if I should find myself halfway across the globe. And the ability to let my friends and family gain access to the media I have stored.

Simple, right? Yeah, didn't think so.

### Post by dracule on 2008-07-09
I am 17. I use Linux for a number of reasons.

It is currently installed on 4 computers in my house as the only operating system on that computer.  I use it for everything. I was surprised to find that it ran my dreamweaver CS3 quite well, so i was very pleased.

I used to defend Windows for a very very long time. I had linux on my computer in 05 and kept it on it till 06. then 2006 came and i got Vista. I foolishly defended vista with all my heart, i even erased linux. I went on and on defending every feature every detail you could say i was a fanboy. Then one day when 7.10 came out, it finally supported my laptops hardware, free and clear. Nothing to hunt down endlessly for, everything just simply worked. I began to use it, and the more I used it, the more effortless it became. Instead of fighting with my computer and me having to download dubious programs to get something customized, everything was right there WANTING to be customized. 

Soon my computer became an extension of me. I began learning so much about computers that I worked extremely hard just to get people to try linux. so far i have gotten approx. 30 people at my school just to install it and use it. they still have windows, but the most important thing is that they still have linux on their hdd. 

Linux forever changed the way I look at a computer. People are there to help you out as you need it and the experience is great. Nothing can make me go back to windows. The one and only reason why it is on my hdd is because of Best Buy and their naive warranty system. 

At school with wubi my roommate finally took the plunge. Wubi makes convincing people to give linux a chance 1000000x easier. 

I really really dislike proprietary software now. like 2 years ago it was quite apparent that it was better than open sourced alternatives. but this past year, it has gotten so much better, it is amazing. Probably in the next year I will be pissing my pants by the state of open sourced software. I hope that it continues to grow and expand into new horizons

---

### Post by Calmor on 2008-07-10
> **OttifantSir said:**
> 
I am hoping for this: Server tucked away, running without notice, a position on the couch viewing HDTV through a PC, or playing a game, a computer that gets turned on when I need to do some work that can save all work to the server, and a laptop that can connect to all of this the moment it's turned on (connected to a network), even if I should find myself halfway across the globe. ### Post by ZarathustraDK on 2008-07-14
I'm pure :lolflag:

I used Ubuntu for all my study-related stuff at the university (Philosophy-studies and Multimedia-studies). Ubuntu (or rather the program DVD::rip) saved my groups asses for our final assignment, which was supposed to be a game based on Casablanca, but ended up being formed into a kind of extra DVD-material using Ren'Py to explore the canonical characters in uncanonical scenarios, thereby learning more about their personality (lol, Casablanca-doujinshi). Anyway, we had huge troubles finding a program to rip the scenes we needed to incorporate into the Ren'Py story, the Windows-guys spend a week on that to no avail (we needed some very specific resolutions and subtitle stuff), every time there was something wrong, subtitles missing, audio was in spanish, or the codec wasn't mpeg1 (which Ren'Py needs). Then I said something like "I can give it a try, I don't know if it'll work though as I haven't tried it before". Next day we had exactly what we needed.

Other than that : Reports (had to use office 2007 through Wine for the degree though, as the rest of the group was using it, damn docx), music, internet, games (Kenta Cho's games rock, and CS Source through Wine once in a while).

I just saw a friends old IBM laptop, chugging along on XP, hideously slow, thousands of apps starting up at startup, 3-4 toolbars in IE. The horror! :-({|=

### Post by Mizzou_Engineer on 2008-07-14
I'm in my early 20s and I have used various flavors of Linux for most things you can do with a computer that are legal and won't upset the missus. If you want a list of the things I've done on Ubuntu or other Linux OSes that I can think of off the top of my head:

- Programming
- School work
- Actual paid work (word processing, spreadsheets, slideshows)
- E-mail, IM, IRC, and Web browsing
- Computer and peripheral hardware diagnostics/repair/recovery (the Ubuntu live CDs are invaluable here)
- Media serving and playback (esp. MythTV)
- Local file and local/remote SFTP/SSH serving
- Backups
- Games
- Photo storage and editing
- CAD
- Electronics/circuits design (e.g. gEDA)
- Virtualization host OS
- Statistical and mathematical work
- Bash scripting (very, very handy)
- Video encoding

Pretty much anything you can think of to do on a computer you can do on Ubuntu or any other free Linux or Unix-like OS. There are very few and very specific things that one cannot do with these OSes, but AFAICT they all involve DRM or using one specific proprietary program or driver with no native *nix version. I can most certainly live without the ability to do those in order to do other things, such as pick out my own new hardware and install the OS on it without the OS refusing to fully function until I call up the company's customer service and beg for a new product activation or code key or put it on Official Vendor-Blessed Hardware Bought Only From The Vendor At Inflated Prices (tm) (R) (C) (we will sue you for copyright infringement for simply reading this and making an unauthorized copy in your brain's memory)

### Post by t0p on 2008-07-19

### Post by tubezninja on 2008-07-19
I just turned 31.

I used WIndows as my primary OS until early 2007 (gee, I wonder what precipitated the change?)

After that, I switched to using "other" operating systems for all my tasks.  Started out with a Mac and OS X, and then decided I had a lot of Windows hardware that I still wanted to get some use out of.  then I decided to give Ubuntu a try.

Now I used ubuntu and OS X 50/50 for desktop usage.  General stuff like surfing the web, e-mail, and dealing code review and the more technical aspects for work end up getting equal time on both machines.  Administering the linux servers gets more time on my ubuntu desktop.  Dealing with the audio/video/photo stuff, and remote administering our last remaining windows server at work, is done on the Mac.

The heavy server stuff though, is almost all linux.  Three SuSE servers, three Ubuntu servers.  Web hosting, large file handling, archiving, and basically all the "heavy lifting" I use linux for.

I do have one Windows desktop left, just in case I absolutely need windows for something.  But it hasn't seen use in weeks.

### Post by AFarris01 on 2008-08-05
20 and 10/12ths, and Ubuntu is my primary OS, which i use to do everything:
Word processing
spreadsheets/budgeting
sound & picture editing (love GIMP!)
bittorent a little bit
programming
web development
managing my extensive collection of music, and my parent's extensive collection of photos
light and heavy gaming, natively and through wine
new App testing (when i can, for wine mostly)
theme designs (nothing published yet, all just for personal so far)
etc... etc...

I was first introduced to Ubuntu by a teaching assistant in one of my college programming classes who happened to have the same first name as me.  He told me to give it a try, and see what i thought.  Fell in love, simple as that.  I re-formatted my desktop about 6 months ago, and since then ive been completely windows free here (the lappy still uses windows, with ubuntu under wubi, only cause its still under warranty, and Geek squad frowns upon people who reformat the hdd on computers you buy from them...)

Ubuntu-er 4 life!

### Post by tjandracom on 2008-08-09
I'm 31 years old. I'm a system administrator and application developer for several companies. In those companies, I use Ubuntu 8.04 as servers and 6.06 as clients whenever possible, and co-exists with Windows servers and Windows clients only when I have to.

In home, I use Ubuntu 8.04 for my daily activities: browsing internet, email, spreadsheets, programming, watch movies and hear music. I use Win XP when I have to develop application that is targeted to be run in Windows.

I think Ubuntu is great, as an alternative operating system. I don't hate Windows, and still think that in few certain points Windows is better (booo....), but I really love the idea that Ubuntu is free and practical (I've tried other Linux distro and I don't think they're practical), and I've been using Ubuntu for my everyday life since Dapper release.

### Post by HappyHenry on 2008-08-15
I'm fifty. I use Ubuntu because, after trying to install many linux distros, I found the Ubuntu forums to be the most friendly knowledgble linux forum available. I dont know that Ubuntu is the, "best," linux distro but I know, Ubuntu has the best community on the planet. That is why i use Ubuntu. I use Linux because after spending thousands of dollars on windonts machines and having to upgrade the OS every year or two the cost just got crazy. And i do mean had to upgrade. The software is written in such a way that you have to have the latest windonts OS. Forget that.
Another reason for linux OS is the idea behind the GPL. In my ideal world knowledge is shared, reasoned, refined and improves life for everyone. Thats GPL in a nutshell. (see my signature)
What do I do with Ubuntu? I serf for, news to rant about in blogs, Art, homeschool research, spread sheets on schooling goals and achievements, games, music. Everything I used to do in windonts and more. More because, I am beginning to understand what this computer can really do with the chains of windonts off of the system AND because my computer is fun to work with again. 
That's the direct, simple and truthful answer, "I have fun with Ubuntu." I had nothing but frustration with other OS's and distros with the exception of Mandrake and Centos. Those two linux distros where pretty nice but the community was nothing for compairing to Ubuntu.
Community, Play, Work, Information, growth.

### Post by Stan_1936 on 2008-09-10
Mid 20s.  Now on to the interesting stuff.......

When I had Windows shoved down my throat, I thought that the word "Mac" was nothing more than a nickname for Mark McGwire.  Needless to say I had no clue about Linicks.  I have gone through (without thinking ofcourse) several Windows operating systems and 2 years ago started removing spare parts from a computer I found on someone's front yard(for garbage disposal presumably) and used them in my old AMD system to spruce it up.  Bought a few more parts and it was "improved/modernized(partially...I only changed some, not all, of the parts I could pull out and push in)" significantly in terms of hardware.  Windows was still not as fast or snappy as it coulda woulda shoulda been on better hardware, but what else was there.  I remember once, while shopping for RAM, seeing a Mac at BestBuy and saying to myself "That's not a computer....who uses that!".  After I understood a bit about some of the hardware components, I saw a guy on TV install Mepis on an old (what he called) "doorstop".  He mentioned that Ubuntu was the most popular version of Linux.....I was introduced to Ubuntu.

Since then I have tested out nearly 20 Linux distros on that old computer.  Sometimes I wake up in the morning and, for no reason at all, I wipe out the hard drive and install a different Linux distro.  Occassionally, I just reformat and re-install the EXACT SAME distro that was on the system the previous night.  I don't know why I do that...perhaps you can tell me?

I use Ubuntu for Office,Internet, Pidgin,email, CDs/DVDs....everything I could do on Windows EXCEPT printing(never figured out that Cups/Samba thing) and burning(old system so no burner).

### Post by xeddog on 2008-09-27
I'm 61 now and use Ubuntu for more and more all the time.  At present I use it for most of my Internet things like IM (Gyachi) to keep in touch with friends from past lives, and current family, Internet (Firefox) for researching how to do different things and also shopping for things that I can't find locally.  OpenOffice for several things including q&d photo editing with Draw (just adding pointers and text, etc.),  Azureus and TED for downloading tv shows (we have since eliminated cable from our list of expenses and just use an antenna),  Mediatomb to serve the tv shows to my D-Link media server that is connected to the telly.  For the time being I still use MS Outlook (NOT Express) for my email but will probably migrate to Thunderbird soon.  And I will probably use Tbird for newsgroups too.

I still have several Windows apps that I have not been able to find compatible (or acceptable) replacements for.  Legacy genealogy software, my Ford truck documentation DVD, Roxio Easy Media Creator, and Pinnacle Studio just for starters.  Btw, there are no Linux versions and none of them work with Wine.  I have tried several different Linux apps that are supposed to do many of these things, but they just lack the ease of use and simplicity of the Windows apps.

I have been involved with computers in one way or another since 1965.  I have tried Linux several times over the years and it just never made it for me.  Just too many things that it either didn't do (or didn't do as well), or took way too much "fussin" to make something work and keep it working.  But now that MS has come out with Vista, I am bound and determined to make Ubuntu work for me.  I tried Vista and I just HATE it.  It won't let me work with the computer the way I want to without tweaking the living excrement out of it.  And after I finish tweaking it, I essentially have XP again so pbtpbtpbtpbtpbtpbt to Vista.

Now I've blabbed too much so . . .

Xeddog

### Post by iheartubuntu on 2008-10-23
> **squaregoldfish said:**
> I'm 923. I use Ubuntu for posting lies on the Internet.

Steve.

So the Nephilim DID come from outer space!! I knew it!

Im 34, in California where everyone here has a Mac. I grew up with windoze (actually DOS first). Got into Ubuntu about a year and a half ago now and LOVE it.

I do have XP in VirtualBox just in case I come across some software that wont work in Ubuntu, but I rarely ever use by VBXP. I had started Ubuntu craze by using Wubi, then migrated to doing a dual boot system, then decided to go all the way with my beautiful Ubuntu and run it as the only native OS now.

I use my system for everything. Email, movies, tv, live soccer feeds using sopcast, skype, pidgin, im liking the new Opera 9.6 browser... what else, ebaying, im writing a book right now in Open Office and suits me well. I love the PDF reader in Ubuntu... so much nicer than Adobe was in XP. I have a number of websites Ive developed and maintain so I have dreamweaver and photoshop working very well in Ubuntu (by using wine doors). I like comics when I get a chance and there is even a nice comic viewer in the repositories. I dont have a lot of time for games, but I have FIFA 04 working through wine, I play Regnum Online once in a while (lvl 14 i think), Frets on Fire is a must for any music lover!, I LOVE Future Pinball and looks brilliant on my nvidia card. Im a sucker for Backgammon and love the "gnubg" version found in Synaptic Packager Manager. You can change board colors, do 3D view.. its awesome. Im stuck on Pogo games lately like Spooky slots and Ali Baba slots which you can play on their website for free. I did get World of Warcraft for my sister working on her new System 76 computer and she loves it.

That reminds me. Ive installed Ubuntu on 20 computers now. My dad, one brother, my sister, several friends, a couple neighbors... They all love it. None of these people are really hard core gamers so it works quite well. My sis plays her WOW game and since I was able to get it going for her, shes happy.

### Post by UGent on 2008-10-24
A late reply but I am a Newbie.

I am 74, not far off 75.  I have installed Ubuntu on my Toshiba laptop, and I just use it for emailing, web surfing etc as one does.

The only snag is that I have not succeeded in getting [Family Historian]("http://www.family-historian.co.uk/") to run under Wine.  I have tried Gramps.  The whole Family History world uses GEDCOM files and Gramps tells you that the import of a GEDCOM will probably result in the loss of information - and they are right !  Thus, *Alan Brian Charles Dobson* will in Gramps become *Alan Dobson*, so if you have a big file there will be a lot of correcting to do.  :(

### Post by eurohim on 2008-10-26
I'm 28 years old and I use Ubuntu on my desktop for web, email, the Terminal Services client, NES Emulation (easier than hooking up my NES), Transmission Bit Torrent and basically everything I used Windows for. I also have a web/ftp server using Ubuntu Server and Apache2 and vsftpd.

I'm trying to figure out how to use it at work (I'm at IT Consultant). Right now I have two Ubuntu Servers loaded with VMware Server so I can test some things like Linux file servers working within Active Directory and XP Clients (works pretty well). I plan to set up an NTP server, perhaps an LDAP server to replace active directory for smaller clients. I'm just trying to figure out where I can get linux to fit in. I also need to find a decent backup program that is equivalent to Backup Exec in many ways (remote agent and whatnot) that is hopefully free.

My clients are primarily Windows-based, but with the economy tanking and the supposed security of Linux, I figured I'd learn it and attempt to incorporate it. I've only been at it for a week or two, but being in IT makes it not that big of a deal to learn.

### Post by ECPCLINUX on 2008-11-15
45 Years of this crazy thing called life! I joined the forum in 2006 two years ago. started using Ubuntu 6.10 (6.04LTS did not work with my then new sata drive). I have three systems, one with Ubuntu 8.10 only, one dual boot with Ubuntu 8.04LTS/XP and the last has a cheap retail copy of Vista Business I picked up from a guy who purchased it and changed his mind (guess he found a pigeon). Purchased it for $80 (yep he took a loss) and $10  to send for the x64 DVD version from M$. I enjoy Ubuntu beyond belief. I anticipate, like a Kid, the new Ubuntu releases. I use my computers for:
0-Fun (Ubuntu)
1-Skype (saves me money)
2-Games (when I'm bit bored, does not happen often)
3-Surf (I specially like to read online, news, forums, emails, & more. Also, listen to podcasts, Music in places like playlist Blah, blah, blah)
4-Pay Bills (Ahhh, the fun never ceases,lol)
6-Work related stuff (open office, spreadsheets, & more)
7-Youtube (videos are just fun to watch,others help me remember the 80's)
8-TVR
9-CHAT!

### Post by texas.chef94 on 2008-11-15
ECPCLINUX your experience prompts this reply. Some absorb the Linux venture with ease and certainly with Ubuntu it has more clarity at least for me. But whether it was my age, being retired and missing my kitchen (was a chef) or maybe like my wife says culinary arts is ALL you understand or want to understand.
To begin with I sent away for Puppy CD and did the loading to ram thing for a while. Started looking at partitioning scared the you know what out me so loaded ubuntu with wubi as a windows file. Played with that, but partitioning was like a (call of the wild) Burned a 8.04 live CD then a gparted. Thought I would partition first and did simply 2 primary's and a swap. Deleted Ubuntu on windows and installed in ext3 I had waiting.
Short version as I lost windows once and had a few other wrecks along way. Its been wild ride but loved every minute. Presently 8.10 with kubuntu desktop because I like Kmenu. Hey coffee is great this morning and enjoyed visit.

### Post by dwasifar on 2008-11-28
46, and I use Ubuntu for everything you would use a computer for:

 - Web, email, & IM
 - Word processing & spreadsheets
 - Audio & video playback
 - Business accounting
 - CD ripping & mp3 management
 - DVD, ahem, "backup"
 - DVDA authoring
 - Blogging
 - Photo editing & storage
 - Shell scripting & development
 - Occasional games
 - General farting around

I do these things across two laptops and two desktop machines, with a new desktop machine currently in build process (awaiting new video card from newegg).  In addition to that, I also have a file server that sits in the basement running Ubuntu.  Besides general file server duties, it also acts as a upnp server for household media streamers.  Plus, I have a sort of hobby of resurrecting old hardware into working systems, which I load up with Ubuntu and sell at bargain prices through supermarket bulletin board ads.

Microsoft is pretty much banished from my house.  There is one Windows machine here - it belongs to a consulting client, and I need it in order to use their VPN, so I have no choice about that.  But otherwise, it's all Linux here.

### Post by snek on 2008-12-03
28

I use Ubuntu on 5 machines now:
- download/fileserver for home = my personal home server, serves to 2 windows machines (intrepid)
- work fileserver = tweaked out gbit fileserver on areca 8 port pci-e 8x raid controller in beautiful black 8 drive hotswap SuperMicro case, serves about 6 workstations for animation studio (hardy server)
- work backupserver @ home = just stores files in a 8 drive software raid setup, backs up the animation studio server nightly (intrepid, didn't have option to get/download hardy)
- work testserver = websites & tweaking tests before it hits the live-server (intrepid, updated from hardy server)
- virtual private server =  website hosting ([www.slicehost.com)(hardy]("http://www.slicehost.com%29%28hardy"))

Ubuntu is great, I used to use Debian but have decided to make the switch all together. The updated packages are a real treat and even the server version I find easier to use. 

Plus the community rocks! 

:guitar:

### Post by chili555 on 2008-12-03

### Post by dmatthys on 2008-12-13
I am 23 and I use Linux for well everything. Knowledge, surfing the web, and school to name a few. Quite frankly even with all the bugs in Ubuntu (and other distro's) that can be found (which most of the time has a fix that I think I have spent no more than two hours finding yet) is still way better then windows. Viruses and spy ware, forget it I download allot of music, I mean allot! I have a 320gb external hard drive that has 11,000 songs and counting; just into music i guess. Windows, I was catching a virus every other week with two paid anti viruses and 1 free as well as two free malware scanners. I haven't had any of those in Ubuntu yet and have been using and downloading for a couple of months now. Not one problem in that dept. Actually the bugs I mentioned earlier about half could be related to my lack of knowledge and mistakes I made the other half were truly bugs and took a little bit of time (about the same as the standard re installation of a windows system) to find a fix and execute it without the destruction of data its just great!! If some aspects of my school didn't require windows I would be rid of it forever.

### Post by OneMixDJ on 2008-12-20
I'm 43 and I use Ubuntu as a means of having my own Linux server and workstation for a change.  


Ubuntu Server 8.04: 

The server has dual NIC interfaces, each on a different /24 VLAN.

What I'm currently doing is really nothing fancy. 
I'm really doing it more for the learning curve and some functionality:

DNS - I'm planning to set up separate domains for my internal networks.
TACACS - I want to use TACACS to connect to my Cisco devices.
OpenSSH - I access the server using the Putty client.
Samba - To access shared drives from my XP machines and my Ubuntu desktop.


Ubuntu Desktop 8.04:

Workstation for simple common things like browsing (Firefox), email (Evolution), IM (Pidgin). I'm also planning to use it for console access to my Cisco devices. Like my server, I'm also running Samba for the shared access.

It would also be ideal to run Audacity on it, but for some reason my audio devices cannot be found so I need to figure out what's going on with that. *Hmmm...*:-k. 

I use my XP machines for things related to DJing, so the Ubuntu desktop is my machine for regular use. 

All in all, it works great! =D>

### Post by Ben Page on 2008-12-21
I'm 24, and I use Linux for everything that it can be used. I love computers and computer software, The biggest reason is that I dont like the direction Winblows is heading to, flashy Vista, Seven, hardware depreciation, software monopol etc. I respect Linux community and their effort, if they only had more support by hardware vendors and software companies, gaming industry, but hey, the goal is profit and selling unnecessary hardware to shallow people that is heavily overpriced.
Linux can do everything that winblows can, but it has compatibility issues with win world. Therefore, I need winblows for some work, I don't want it, but thanx to large pirate community witch is as great as Linux community, Winblows and its apps are also free. (I have over 3000 dollars worth of software on my PC that I never payed, and never will - take that Billy!)
To answer the Q :), i use it to surf, dnld torrents, burn DVDs, gaming (limited), work (write, presentations, base), turn my PC to HT (in Winblows my tvout don't work, but Linux xconf and terminal made posible to write some code and to make my overpriced nvidia card to work on composite PAL big screen TV, with winblows I would have to buy a HDTV) so not only its free, it saved me 500 dollars at least!

### Post by ivaarsen on 2009-01-06
26, and a general pack rat.  I own 9 computers, and am in possession of 3 more for work purposes.  They include 2 server machines (my server machine runs 8.04 [file shares, network services, etc]), 3 laptops (one's an Intel Mac), the rest are desktops (one is a PowerPC Mac).  My Vaio desktop will always have Windows on it because, sadly, I lose hardware functionality without it.  ...And my main work machine (one of the laptops) needs to have Windows on it too.  :(

Ubuntu is my primary operating system at home, so I use it for pretty much everything.  However, I'm an all-around tinkerer, so on some machines the installed operating system changes frequently based on my needs.  And I am one of those people who needs change in my operating system every once in a while.  You know, to keep it fresh and stay sharp.  That said, I'll use OSX and other Linux distros (mostly k/xubuntu) in spurts.

That's my story, stickin' to it.  V,,

### Post by hawkhock on 2009-01-06
I am 67. Started with Wubi Ubuntu 8.10 in November 2008 to learn how to use it. Within two months, fully installed Ubuntu and terminated Windows OS. Primary reason for complete switch was continuing problems with Windows programs upgrade costs, OS bugs with ongoing fixes, security issues. 

Before switching, I researched Linux package replacements for all my software programs. I was able to replace all but one program. So am using Ubuntu for all applications that I used in Windows i.e. email, web browsing photo editing, newsletter writing, databases, spreadsheets, etc. I've had a few problems but forum users have provided the answers to all my questions so far.  My computer is old but running better in Ubuntu than it ever did in Windows with fewer problems.  Very pleased so far.

### Post by Virtualboxbuntu on 2009-01-14
> **4E.Kipper said:**
> 21 years young.
switched to ubuntu after having LOADS of problems with XPmediaC. (as in, constant blue screens while doing nothing in particular, corrupt files, useless tech support etc)
only been using it for about 1-2 months and i just use it for general use as my graphics card seems faulty and cant hold a straight edge when playing games.

i intend to get a new computer....and, well, guess whats going on that?  saves me around £100 cos of not buying and OS. (will eventually get windows, but mainly for "just in case")

i also fancied learning to programming langauges and the command line (it still scares me as to what can be done with it, are they actually any limits?)

Wow, over 1,000 replies already!

If you are using the command line as a normal user (not the root account) then you're pretty safe. But if you are logged in as root, there are no limits, and you can easily mess something up if you aren't careful.

I'm glad that so many people use Linux!

### Post by chinasteve2000 on 2009-02-13
> **David Ostrom said:**
> Just curious. What is your age and what do you use Ubuntu for?
Me? I'm 40, surf the web, e-mail, and gimp. I also like to install and uninstall programs from the repository, trying to brake and fix the system. I find that is the best way to learn.


I'm 34, I have used Ubuntu for 3+ years because I couldn't get an OEM XP install CD for my Toshiba Tecra 2100. Ever since, I have grown to love the challenge, freedom, and community of doing almost everything on Ubuntu. 

I use Ubuntu to do everything (including all David wrote above) but video editing, Palm sync'ing, and making DVD menus (All of which I use OSX for, avoiding Windows most of the time). Right now I'm looking for a good backup program for Ubuntu via Synaptic so I can synchronize with my backup before my hard drive fails. Then I'll get back to that Toshiba.

Presently, I'm on our Macbook, is running 8.04 also because it's hard drive failed too and I haven't found the restore disk. My wife handles Ubuntu just fine in the mean time...and I'm kinda enjoying the accelerated graphics and fun I can have with this machine. Almost all the hardware is set up now, and most of it worked right away from a fresh Live CD install!

Truthfully, I find Ubuntu more easy to manipulate than any other OS, though the few things it is missing OSX more than makes up for. Too bad OSX doesn't make itself completely compatible with repositories in the open source community! Do that with simplicity and I'll always have Mac and Ubuntu on two computers in my house! :)

### Post by Lifelock on 2009-03-11
I am 31 years old and  I use ubuntu as my every day operating system for work and personal activities.

I am a computer scientist, so at work I use ubuntu for all research activities: mathematical analysis and simulations (maxima and scilab), writing articles (latex/texmaker/jabref), software design and programming (argouml/eclipse), presentations and lecturing (openoffice), teleconferencing (skype) and a bunch more.

It is interesting to see how the use of operating systems has changed in my field (software engineering) over the years. A couple of years ago, it seemed windows had a clear advantage over all others (not necessarily in general, just what I saw at conferences). Right now, apple is making a strong push with their os x and sleek macbooks. The use of linux is also somewhat increasing, but not as much.

With the increasing capabilities in exchanging files and file formats, cross-platform applications and the relatively lower dependency on ms office in research, it seems the choice of os is more one of personal preference. All of them seem to get the job done. I prefer working in linux though.

### Post by skeebo on 2009-03-19

My choice was simple.

### Post by Screwdriver0815 on 2009-03-28
I am 33 and I have two Computers at home.

The Desktop currently has Intrepid Ibex installed, but gets a new installation of Jaunty Jackalope when it is released.
So the Desktop is the experimental machine and gets every new release of Ubuntu.

On the Laptop also Intrepid Ibex is installed but it will remain there until the end-of-support has come.

I use Ubuntu for everything I need. This is internet, e-mails, printing, scanning, Gimp-ing, creating and modifying pdf-files, webradio, web-TV, gaming, organising photos and music... and so on.

This is more than I did with windows. Reason is: windows was annoying and far away from being a desktop experience and fun to use.
Ubuntu is... its a desktop experience, its nice, its fun to use and provides much more possibilities to the user to do new things.

The next step for me is learning shell script programming, but I am still far from that as I am still struggeling a bit with the shell commands. But I like to use the shell and as soon I have enough time I will start the learning procedure for the programming.

I also use it for translating because I am member of the german translators group for Ubuntu. So also contribute to it and try to give at least a little bit back to make Ubuntu the best operating system.

### Post by callie510 on 2009-04-05
I am 23, a senior in college, and I have been using ubuntu for 6 months. I use it as my main system - mostly I use Ubuntu to procrastinate my thesis, browse the web, watch things on Hulu, update Facebook, and sometimes I do some schoolwork on it too using OpenOffice. (I switched to OpenOffice thanks to Ubuntu; before that I was completely Microsoft Office!) I also use the terminal to break my computer about once a week.

The only thing I use every day that just doesn't have an Ubuntu equivalent is iTunes - I spent months trying all the Linux music programs, and not a single one correctly & reliably syncs ratings and playcounts between computer & iPod as simply as itunes does. So I always keep a VirtualBox window open on a second desktop with my iTunes library open, to sync. :) Otherwise I am 100% linux.

### Post by Roasted on 2009-04-05
22. I've been using Ubuntu since 6.06.

I use it as my main OS, however I dual boot cause I play a little bit of PC games now and then. I also utilize Ubuntu with Samba so I have a file sharing system set up, and I have SyncBack free edition on all of the XP computers in the house which at 330AM and 330PM automatically synchronizes the computer's "my documents" folder to the specified Samba share on my network drive in my Ubuntu machine. In essence, I have a backup server running within my typical desktop.

I also use Ubuntu at work heavily with the use of FOG, an open source cloning utility that runs on top of Fedora or Ubuntu, but I chose to run on Ubuntu. I use it daily because out of the 1800 computers we have, every day there seems to be at least 4 or 5 that need reimaged, whether it be a virus or corrupt file. It's just easier to blast the image out and relink the user's files from the server back to the computer. 

I utilize Ubuntu and any/all open source software I can find before I resort to using anything else at work. Being in IT support you get to play around with a lot of programs, so it's nice having a dual boot laptop @ work with XP/Ubuntu, but I find myself in Ubuntu more, whether I'm imaging or network troubleshooting.

---

### Post by GepettoBR on 2009-04-09

> I'm 20 years old, turning 21 next week (ugh >.<).  I use Ubuntu to play 90% of my games under Cedega, play music, surf the web, check e-mail, and yadda-yadda.  I only use Windows to play my MMORPGs that I can't run under Cedega which are Cabal, Last Chaos, Perfect World, and Project Torque.

### Post by 16sinker on 2009-04-17
I just found this thread and have not read the whole thing. I did however go back 5 days or so, and I discovered that I'm a little bit older than the average user. I am 61, believe it or not. I had never used a computer until 4 years ago and it was this computer that I am using now. At that time it ran Windows Millenium Edition and used I.E. It also used a dialup connection that was quite slow. I was captured-I soon heard about Firefox and downloaded it. I then started using Stumbleupon and soon ran across Linux, Ubuntu and open source. I also found out that these open-source operating systems ran well on old equiptment. I chose Ubuntu. Started with Gutsy without success and then upgraded ram from 256mb to 512mb on this late ninetys model of the Dell Inspiron 8000. I finally got Hearty Heron to load but had random freeze-up issues until Intrepid Ibyx came along. I took the release candidate of that distrbution and it's been running great for the last 6 months. Two weeks ago I took a beta release of Jaunty 9.04, have taken updates since then and have only had one bug to deal with. It was taken care of in the later updates and I'm proud to say that I'm running Jaunty beta on this old computer and it is doing great. To make this long story short, I like to resurrect these old machines and Ubuntu works best-Ive already told you my age.

### Post by Shibblet on 2009-05-01
I'm 33.  I use Ubuntu on an MSI Wind Netbook.  I use it for digital photography, watching movies, surfing the web, email, etc.  All of the things computers are used for.

I don't dislike Windows in its entirety, but there are many things I expect from an Operating System that Windows doesn't deliver.

1. Boot time.  And I'm not talking about from BIOS to Explorer, I'm talking about watching Explorer load up all of the taskbar items.  (which can partially be contributed to Microsoft, and partially contributed to the driver manufacturer).

2.  Simple tasks, like copying or erasing a file, put you into limbo.  i.e.  I just emptied the recycle bin, there are only two files in it, what could possibly be taking so long.

3.  SPEED!  On a Netbook you have very limited system resources.  1.6Ghz Atom Processor, 1 GB RAM, 80GB HD, Intel Extreme Graphics.  Ubuntu makes my Netbook work like a high end machine.  Task switching, program loading, watching videos, playing music, photos, surfing, etc.  All run the way a PC is supposed to.

### Post by nm4m on 2009-05-02
I am 48 years old.

Ubuntu my main OS at home.  Surf, email, videos, open office, some web work, but it is all done in text editor.  

I have a windows computer for work, and one for ham radio activities.  There is some ham radio linux stuff out there, but nothing that matches DX Labs, and some of the other software, such as monitoring Weather radar and for programing radios and scanners.  Ham radio contesting is another big piece of the activity with that computer.

Otherwise, I can use Ubuntu for everything.  I will be setting up an Ubuntu server box (currently running Debian 4) that will be running LinuxRMS an access point for AX.25 packet for Emergency Communications.  That works real well on Debian, and folks are having great luck with it on Ubuntu, but have not switched yet.

Started out playing with RedHat 2.1 or so.  Back when (to me) it was a big accomplishment to get X11 running, and never could print or get to the internet.  So played with it off and on.  Was really liking Caldera there for a while.  Pre-lawsuit, and worked better out of the box than RedHat.

Then Windows XP came out, and the activation ticked me off.  I was on Win2k at the time.  So moved full time to Mandrake (name before Mandriva).  Then to Fedora.  The to Debian for a bit.  The Ubuntu.

In fact I have had this box for at least 5 years, and it has never had Windows on it.  It started out life with Fedora and is now running Ubuntu.  Though after the upgrade to 9.04 Firefox no longer works, but still digging into that.  Great site, thanks to all for the help.

Dave
NM4M

### Post by michaelpagz on 2009-05-03
I'm 26 and I use Ubuntu essentially as a windows replacement. I am a just above average computer user who basically had a beef with pop ups and trojans and was getting tired of cleaning up computers of family members. I knew about linux and decided to give it a blind try and I haven't looked back since. 
At the beginning of each upgrade I bare the brunt of about a week of fixing the bugs on about 8 different computers that I converted to Ubuntu of friends and family and then its smooth sailing! Everyone loves it and I love it because I don't have to deal with "My computer is broke. The internet said I had a virus," or other typical gripes. 
On that note, I have to say, every new release has been increasingly easier to use and fix upon upgrade.  Even now with my unfortunate combo of 9.04 and ATI radeon x600, it still looks and functions awesomely.
I know people say its clunky or its too big for its britches now, or that its turning into another windows, but Ubuntu offers something that other OSes, linux or other wise, don't: a real world alternative to windows and mac. Some of the least tech-savy people I have ever met now use Ubuntu (don't worry, they come to me for help, not the forum.hehe.) I know to some that is everything wrong with Ubuntu, but to me its the thing that sets Ubuntu out above the rest.

Sorry about my posi-rant! I appreciate this forum and everything everyone involved with Ubuntu does! Keep of the good work!

---

### Post by skotos on 2009-05-04
Turning 45 in a few days. I have been running GNU/Linux since its early days... I think the first kernel version I remember as such was 0.6x/0.7x or something like that (it was around 1992-93 and all floppy disks based).

My interest for Linux is professional: since its early days I have been looking for a cheap way to practice with the Unix environment at home. In the 80's and in the 90's Unix was really expensive and prohibitive for an individual. IBM AIX, Sco System V, Xenix and the other *nix dialects were sold for lots of bucks and the hardware they were running on was often specialized and definitely more expensive than a regular PC at that time disguised as OS/2 compatible... **LOL**

I currently run Ubuntu as a system engineer *to check the networks, to run different types of servers* (for file sharing, web access and services, security, VPNs, multimedia, ...) and *to develop* in a stable environment with *Eclipse*.

At home I do appreciate its multimedia commodities: I daily plug my notebook to the TV *to watch movies* and *pictures. *I *listen podcasts* with *gpodder and listen to music and internet radios* with *amarok* (btw amarok is the greatest music player ever developed and currently available)*...*

Since I have experience as a computer graphics expert and I am working as web developer too, I appreciate *Gimp* that is becoming - day by day - more and more powerful and attractive (and a true PhotoShop competitor) and I am practicing with *inkscape* as well.
I run *sane* to scan my documents, *OpenOffice* to write down maths formulas and to create PDF files I have to share with others, *GeoGebra* to draw high school graphs and charts, *Truecrypt* to protect the documents I travel with...

My notebooks jump onto the internet in all the possible modes: 56k modem, lan, wireless. GNU/Linux has always been in fact far better than Windoze in taking care of multiple network configurations and does it in zero time...

I *study* and *improve myself* using screencasts and video tutorials. I *read* .chm, .pdf, .cbr and .cbz files for work and in leasure time: my eletronic library has now turned 60 Gb.

I use 5 desktops with *Compiz* in a regular *dual monitors configuration* to manage the many tasks I try to accomplish in a multithreaded human environment.

I regularly login to my *bank account* from Ubuntu, pay *taxes* on the net and sometimes buy some oddities.

Well, the list of the things Ubuntu helps me doing might go on... and probably too much of my life depends on computers, but I cannot think of a life without them any more...

Since Windoze had always been professionally annoying me with serial numbers, expensive options, outdated applications, viruses, cracks, absurd behaviours, blue screen of deaths and my life is in front of a keyboard for so many hours a day Linux has become more and more friendly to me because of its affordability, options and cost.

*Ubuntu captured me with its live cd* and I immediately got married to it: I am installing it on every device I own and that I will ever buy (currently *mobile phones* and *eeepcs*).

Since there are not yet Ubuntu specialized versions for routers and access points I install *openwrt*, dreaming of it for the future...

In the end, whatever I did in the last three years, I did it with UBUNTU! 

[B]Thank you, Richard S. !!
Thank you, Mark S. !![/B]

---

### Post by drlmg on 2009-05-04
I am 42 and I use Ubuntu for everything I do on all my computers, home and office. the one exception is the patient scheduling and medical practice software at the office. The server and workstation computers run M$ 2000 only because it will not run on anything but M$. I run the program on Ubuntu via Wine but it is limited. I use Linux for digital photos and video both personal and that I DL, news, e-mail, documents, music, satellite maps, and tons of other things I cannot readily think of. I love Ubuntu, the only complaint is that half the time I update one of my laptops it completely fails to work. In the past (7 and earlier) I was always able to restore and / or recover. With the unpredictable kernel updates I cannot do that anymore on the laptops, I simply do not update them. I wait for a stable LTS version and upgrade the whole OS.

### Post by keithpeter on 2009-05-09
Hello All

**Age**: when I was in the 6th form, we used a teletypewriter and acoustic coupler to write BASIC programs on a University mainframe. We saved our homework on punched paper tape. The modem was in a 19 inch rack and had a rotary dial.

**What do you use Ubuntu for?** :Mainstream computing tasks (e-mail, Web, Moodle admin at the College where I teach, handouts in OpenOffice, presentations in Impress. The OpenOffice mathematical formula editor is one of the best I've used apart from TeX). 

**The software I miss most from the Mac OS world**: Tinderbox, by Eastgate Systems, totally unique and deep, and Preview, the Mac OS pdf reader that can edit and export snippets of a PDF page.

### Post by fletchoid on 2009-05-09
I am 55.  I use Ubuntu Jaunty 9.4 64 bit on my main computer mainly as a means of evolving away from Windows.  I use it for email, web surfing, some word processing and downloading and watching TV shows, YouTube, etc.  I am just starting to learn how to install some of my older games (Prey, Half Life series).  I also use Ubuntu 8.04 32 bit on an older PC to play my TV shows on the TV.  When I am doing multiple things at once, I like the ability to have numerous independent desktops available.  On one desktop, I can have music playing and a browser open to find info on the artist playing.  On another desktop, I can be using Open Office and Firefox to research and write on a topic.  On another desktop, I can have my email and Pidgin open, so I can be keeping in touch with people. I also find Ubuntu usefull when XP crashes and I have to access files on my NTFS drives. In XP, if ONE lousy file in a folder is corrupted, you cannot access ANY of the files in that folder.  I just go into Ubuntu, and delete the corrupt file, and I can now access all the rest of the files in XP, no problem.  Learning to use the terminal was a bit of a challenge, but I am getting used to it.  My only real complaint is that I would like more of my games to work on Ubuntu.  Some of them work okay, but there are still a lot of bugs in the gaming aspect of Ubuntu.

---

### Post by msandoy on 2009-05-09

### Post by justinmiller87 on 2009-05-11

### Post by grungedoobie on 2009-05-15
I'm 33, and have been using Linux for well over two years.  Started with Edgy (or was it Feisty) and after the few kinks are worked out with Jaunty will switch to it full time (kinks are not Jaunty's fault in my case, the fault is between Gateway, M$ and China).  I've used Linux to diagnose-repair-devirus M$ computers, restore trashed drives and lost files, run servers (still learning but at least have Samba under a little control, she's so powerful it's hard to tame her), video editing, audio editing, photo editing, even midi editing (seems to be a dying art), tons of web surfing, gaming and more.  With M$, I never knew a computer could do so much without crashing.


The way of the Penguin means freedom to me.  A huge "THANK YOU!" to all the people around the world who have contributed to such a wonderful gift to humanity.  ):P 


The Grunge.

### Post by skotos on 2009-05-18
> **nicolaw1 said:**
> I am 19 and use Kubuntu for everything, but because of limited knowledge of Linux I am going to change to Ubuntu soon which I hope will be easier to use.
Sorry nicolaw1, but that will not change. Kubuntu runs over KDE, while Ubuntu on Gnome: both of them are window managers but, as such, they do not change what lies behind the scenes. Operating systems are a complex matter to fully manage as an expert.

What makes GNU/Linux and - in our case - [K]ubuntu the OS of choice is not the simplicity (again, no OS is simple), but its consistency, speed, freedom, almost complete absence of viruses, and many other qualities we all talk about.

Do not even forget that a system needs to be affordable in order for you to trust it: this is our case. Go on with the [K]ubuntu flower you like best and - in the medium and long term - you will be happy to have just scratched the surface of a free universe.

---

### Post by arkiedan on 2009-05-18
I'm 71 (an official old guy!) and reasonably computer literate. I enjoy building and updating the household computers so I thought I'd play around with Linux. I use Ubuntu (9.04 now) for everything online, including visiting photo, firearm (yep, I shoot handguns for fun,) computer forums, purchasing about everything, maintaining my two Harmony remote controls, genealogy research and maintaining my own genealogy website. Open Office for writing my short stories and anything document related. I suppose I use this great operating system for most everything come to think of it.

Been wanting to get away from Microsoft for years and went to Ubuntu about a year ago, after trying several Linux operating systems. I hate the fact that I desperately need Legacy, a Windows-only genealogy application, so I run XP in Virtualbox for that program alone.

Okay! I know! Too much information. Sorry.

arkiedan

### Post by StormDrain on 2009-05-19
**Re: What is your age and what do you use ubuntu for?**             
                                                       
Half a century... Where did it all go? 

I remember my first computer with a 8086 and a  8087 math coprocessor. 
I remember 2400 baud modems and using SlipKnot over the university computer to get internet access.  ](*,)

I remember dimm's, dipp's, simm's and sipp's. 

I remember MFM and RLL. And the expression that came across a customers face when you converted their 20Mb MFM hard drive to a 30Mb RLL, increasing its capacity by 50% and increasing its read write times to boot, (metaphorically speaking).

Now I clunk around the internet with Ubuntu on a 900 mhz AMD, doing my online auction and virtual store business. :-({|=


                                  Ok, its past 1 AM and time for the old people to go beddy bye. :)

### Post by yo_pandabear on 2009-06-08
I have Ubuntu (Kubuntu) installed on 3 machines.  All three have removable SATA HD and one or more installed HD for extra storage.  With an extra HD you can use the spare HD as trial & error and not worry about not having a working PC should you have a disaster.  Changing HD's is as quick as pluging in a power cord.

I now run XP Pro on only one machine.  This due to the fact that several accounting programs will not run with Wine.

My first computer was in 1983.  In 1983 I thought I was an old man.  I am now 72 years of age.   

At my age, I have more young friends that old ones.  I make sure all of them know about Linux.  Spread the word!

Cheers

### Post by Jekshadow on 2009-06-08
I'm 14 and use Ubuntu for everything (0 Windows computers). On my laptop, I use it for schoolwork (writing papers, research, etc), programming, graphics, web surfing, gaming (WINE all the way!) and syncing my iPhone (well, using a custom script to connect and then sync my ~/Music, ~/Videos, ~/iPhoneApps and my jailbroken iPhone over SSH). On my server/desktop I use it as a file server (both SAMBA for inter-network and FTP for remote access over the internet), web server (not on the internet, just to test things like PHP), and other things, like application/script testing (for example, testing a script that shreds your entire home directory and then creates normal looking files to hide a shred).

Parents still use XP SP2 and Vista... They still could not tell you what Linux was other than "An operating system".

### Post by sp00f3r on 2009-06-12
I'm  31 years. I use Ubuntu on 3 machines:
[LIST=1]
[*]Mythbuntu (9.04) on my HTPC in the living room for PVR, streaming web video, music, etc. - Just started few weeks ago, so it's a WIP.

### Post by rkirk on 2009-06-20
20 years old.

I use Ubuntu for everything, except for when I want to play around with a BSD, of course. And, once in a blue moon, I have to help someone with their Windows box (natch). But to be more specific than that and give a general rundown of computer usage:

**programming**{Java, python, Common Lisp, etc.}
**webdesign**{and BASH scripting makes this *so much* easier.}
**communicating**{Ekiga for VOIP, irssi for IRC, and Firefox for forums (natch).}
**listening to music (and podcasts)**{either VLC or sox(1)'s "play" command.}

Of course, I could do all these things in Windows (my former OS), I suppose, but I've never used IRC in Windows, and the only programming I did in it was python (over two years ago). I've never even *compiled a program* in Windows and I wouldn't know where to start, so I guess I **need** some sort of Linux to program at all.

### Post by james.wilde on 2009-07-07
****!  Is it only youngsters in here?

I'm 65 and I use Ubuntu for nearly everything I do on the computer: reading the newspapers, paying my bills, booking tickets, getting information, downloading films, music, tv and radio programs, some of this legal, writing (novels in Open Office), keeping records of our "farm", bookkeeping, updating a couple of simple websites, chatting to my relatives, planning political activities for the Swedish Pirate Party...

So come on, you youngsters, there's a challenge for you!

:)

23.
