### Post by dylbud on 2009-07-10
I'm 35, I have Ubuntu installed on my Dell laptop, along with XP. I use Ubuntu for surfing, email (I use Thunderbird for email), creating office documents with OpenOffice (spreadsheets, letters, presentations). I use GIMP and Inkscape (mostly Inkscape) to do art and design work. I've been trying to use some of the audio and video editors, but I can't seem to figure them out.

I also use Ubuntu to learn about Linux and computers in general. There's a lot you can learn from just fooling around on it, and reading EVERYTHING in the menus and dialogs. I would like to help others learn about it and use. To that effect, I am doing a workshop at my local county fair this summer on Intro to Ubuntu.

I would like to learn more about using the terminal and command lines, but I also want to explore how to do high-level stuff through the GUI only. For example, many "how-to"s often instruct you to install packages with the terminal, but those same packages can often be installed with the Synaptic Package Manager.  In teaching others how to use Ubuntu, I want to show them how to use the GUI whenever possible, and only go to the terminal when there is no other way.

### Post by Roger Steiner on 2009-07-19

### Post by a3uge on 2009-08-04
I'm 20. I've been using Ubuntu for 4 years, and 3 years with no dual booting whatsoever. I use it for school, despite warnings from my school to get Windows XP, because everything else has low support. Ran into very few problems, despite being an Engineering and then a CS major. My first desktop was a small Shuttle XPC, used that for a year and a half, worked good for a dorm. Upgraded to a bigger and faster PC when size was no longer an issue and have Mythubuntu up on the shuttle now. Recently bought a Netbook, and of course, it has Ubuntu Netbook Remix on it (But left XP on it just in case a professor decides to be a butthead and make us use a program not supported in Linux).

### Post by Katalog on 2009-08-15
> **mike67114 said:**
> Only drawback is that I still haven't learned to BACKUP my stuff!!!

Hey Mike. Backing stuff up isn't really that difficult. I just copy/paste all the important data from my Home folder (gpg keys, Documents folder, bookmarks, and export my settings and data from Evolution, and other configuration folders for my favorite apps like Empathy) into it's own folder, compress and encrypt the folder and put the encrypted/password protected file in my Dropbox (which if you haven't signed up for it is very handy: [http://www.getdropbox.com/](http://www.getdropbox.com/) , you get 2GB of free online storage), as well as placing it on an external drive that I keep at work in case my place ever burns down, computer explodes, etc. After all, it's mostly the data that's in your Home folder that you're really going to need to get back if you ever have to reinstall Ubuntu or replace your machine.

If you want to use a program to automate the process or perhaps make it a little easier and requires less intervention on your part, you might want to go to "Accessories>Add/Remove Applications" and look for an application called "Simple Backup". I don't use it myself, but it is written specifically for Ubuntu and from what I understand it is relatively easy to use.

And speaking of staving off Alzheimer's (I'm 46) I have a recurring event with an alarm set up in my Evolution calendar to remind me to back up my data once a week. Othewise I'd go for weeks and forget all about it. :P

### Post by Janeleaper on 2009-08-28
I'm 56 and I've been using Ubuntu for more than a year.

I use Open Office, word processor and spreadsheet, for correspondence and writing. I don't find OO as good as Microsoft Office, but it is good enough. I also use Google Documents for processing.

I use GRAMPS for genealogy.  I found it more difficult to get the hang of than the Windows genealogy software I'd been using, but having mastered it I've found that it will do more.

I use GNU for household accounts.  It is very good for keeping records, but I miss Microsoft Money's budget tool.  I've made my own using figures from GNU plus a spreadsheet, but it is not as good.

I use F Spot Photo Manager, which is OK - ish.  I never seem to quite get the hang of it.  My primary storage of photos is on Flickr and I only use my PC as back up. I must try harder. If I want to find a picture on my PC then I find it quickest using Picasa. I also use the GIMP editor occasionally.

And of course I browse the web.  I"m a home-sick Brit, living in Canada, so I spend a lot of time on the BBC website, listening to BBC radio, either on Gecko, or the BBC IPlayer.

Oh and I play a lot of Solitaire.

### Post by ConfederateColonel on 2009-09-09
56 (I learned programming sitting at a big keypunch machine where we typed code onto Hollerith cards aka "IBM cards", gave the stack of cards to the girl at the window and waited for the program to be run on the mainframe. We'd get the results on  "green bar" paper.)

I have owned a software company since 1995 - we produce software that political campaigns use to file their reports with the government. We currently have 3 PCs in the office running Ubuntu, and have 4 more waiting to be switched over as soon as we have the time. As long as we're in business, we will continue to run Windows because that's what our customers use.

We're looking for an alternative to Microsoft and hopefully Linux will do the job. At this point though, we use them mainly for the OpenOffice suite, email and web browsing. We haven't yet started programming on any of the Linux machines - mostly just experimenting and getting used to the OS. We do programming - not hardware or OS, so we're pretty much newbies when it comes to figuring out Linux. I haven't done command line stuff for so long that it's almost a bit scary.

### Post by Katalog on 2009-09-11
> **westmi said:**
> 40,I use it for everything but the darn majicjack.
I am not a good user yet,don't know how to write drivers that would allow mj on linux. keep getting the run-around from mj president about having to have micro$oft

Never heard of "majicjack", but have you tried running it under WINE, or in VirtualBox? Failing that, maybe you could find someone in one of the multimedia sections of the forums who may be able to help either point you to some drivers, or (depending on how solvent you are) perhaps you could offer a small bounty to try and get a kindly developer to write drivers for it if they don't already exist. Probably the easiest thing you could do is submit it as an idea in [Brainstorm]("http://brainstorm.ubuntu.com/") stating that Ubuntu needs to be able to support this (application, device, not sure what it is?) and if it gains enough traction it could actually end up getting included in or added to Ubuntu.

EDIT: Googling around a bit, it seems that there are some Ubuntu users who have succesfully gotten this working under VMWare.

### Post by SergeiSoares on 2009-09-13

### Post by badger_fruit on 2009-09-21
Age 33 at last count :)

My current setup:-
1. XP  "Client PC" as Media centre in main lounge --> Sony 32" Bravia TV
2. Suse11.0 "Client PC" as Media centre in Bedroom 1 --> 19" ACER TFT
3. Suse10.3 "Client PC" as Media centre in Bedroom 2 --> 21" Unbranded CRT
4. Suse 11.0 "Laptop PC" as Media centre

5. Suse 11.0 "Server PC" as file server (1 x 2TB LVM) "TV Series"
6. Suse 11.0 "Server PC" as file server (1 x 1.5TB LVM, 1 x 500GB) "Movies", "Music" and "Music Videos"
7. Suse 11.0 "Server PC" as Webserver, Mailserver, Nagios
8. Windows XP "Server PC" as Download PC (1 x 250gb) - all machines "save as" to this machine then AV and distributed accordingly
9. Ubuntu 9.04 "Server PC" as MythTV & recordings

All behind 10. Smoothwall firewall
20mbit cable connection
very high electricity bill!

Want to get rid of both XP machines although XP Client PC is used for some games and I can't be bothered with WINE .. there's also an issue where Flash player is very jumpy under Suse for some reason on this machine (and only this machine!)

As for XP Server PC, again, this could be gotten rid of once I can get VNC working correctly under Suse (as I am more at home with that) - and yes, I am sure it's easy enough to do but it's one of those "if it ain't broken..." situations!

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2009-09-21
I'm 21, and I use Ubuntu with Gnome, e17 on the old family computer, and we use it for plenty of things with my family.

However, I personally use it for my 3d programs I got through a local student program (Cinema 4D and 3ds max, both Windows versions that work better in Linux, much faster), and I develop for IMVU, which I also use regularly in Linux. Wine's a wonderful thing.

I also frequently use GIMP for 3d texturing, web design with 'slices', and I use tons of native Linux apps for everything from RSS feeds to music management to musical composition. And it's all fast, beautiful, and easy.

I just love Linux. So many solutions to every feasible 'problem' (since all applications are just solutions to problems), and it's all well-integrated, fast, secure, and free.

What more could one ask for?

### Post by Georgia boy on 2009-09-21
55. Email, Internet, Gimp, Pidgen, Photos, Music etc. Still learning and enjoying. I think that it's a great system. Yes, different from Windows but wouldn't expect to act like Windows. Knew that before the dual boot setup and still wanted to get into the system. Learned alot and still have alot to learn. Not shy about asking either. Have explained Ubuntu to family and friends. Not preaching nor knocking down Windows to them. Just giving them an alternative to check out and see if they would like to use or not. Explained to them that it's not for everyone but that I wanted to let them know that they do have options out there to try. Explained about the Open Source products available and more. Maybe they will check it out and like it and then again maybe not but they now know that they have other options to explore. I've found this to be a great bunch to work with and also to learn from. Constantly dropping into the forums to see what is going on and learning from the experiences that others are having. 

Tom

### Post by dchurch24 on 2009-10-13
(bugger, posted this as an almost exact same thread)

I'm 37.

In my house I have a touchscreen attached to a machine running Xubuntu. My main TV is ubuntu 8.04 running MythTV. This machine has an ADU200 unit plugged into it and also an 8-way USB relay.

Also on that machine it runs a Python TCPIP listener that listens for commands to execute.

On the touchscreen machine, I can touch a "button" - it's SDL so not really a 'button' more of a 'sprite' - and turn the lights on, turn the amp on, return the Mythbox to the menu, open the front door etc...

I also have a MyBookWorld that is hacked and running linux that I use as a file server (it's not running Ubuntu, that would probably kill the poor 32mhz processor!).

I have another box running 8.04 running MythFrontend - that also has a 8 way relay plugged into it. On that one however, there is a wireless remote-control that I have opened up and shorted the connections into the relay switches. The remote control is one from those kits you can buy for around 10 quid where you can remotely turn on and off power sockets. This way, I can remotely turn off lamps, amplifiers etc... without wires. The lamps in that room ARE wired as there was little point wasting the wireless plugs when the lamp was right next to the PC/Relay.

I also have another 8.04 box (server edition) that hosts a MySQL DB with tables that hold the wherabouts of each device that can be controlled.

So, each 'client' can talk to each 'server' and vice-versa.

To find which machine controlled the front door, I would simply have the client do this:

"select * from automation where room='front' and device='door' and action='on';"

The IP address of the machine with the relay that controls the door would be returned along with the command to switch the correct relay (./adu sk2 for example).

On my *big* machine, I have a VM setup using VirtualBox running Trixbox with a free SIP *in* number. I have real exentions set up so that the mythboxes can be called. Each mythbox has a Centronix wireless headset and mic, so you can answer the phone from anywhere in the house, but callers can be directed through to whichever room they want (provided it's one that has a Mythbox - there are 4 in this house).

The added advantage of this is that I can have 'secret' exentions that I can call that execute Python scripts to lookup which machine to execute the commands on. This way, if, for instance I forget my keys and leave them indoors, I can simply phone up from my mobile phone and tap in the 'secret' exention that opens the front door, and voila! I'm back in the house.

I can control each and every device through cron or through a command line, so even when abroad I can turn lights on and off, open the door if there's someone who wants to drop of a parcel etc... I even have a 'holiday' mode which randomizes the times that lights come on and turn off within a given time frame so that the house looks occupied and without any pattern that might be recognised.

Just recently, I have started work on my 'whole house audio' which is basically a media centre that I have written in Mono/SDL that checks a central playlist table (on the MySQL server) for songs to play. Each song will have a 'room id' attached to it, so each machine running the media centre knows if it is to play that song, or wait for another. On the touchscreen box a user can select which room they want the music to play in - if they don't choose one it defaults to the living room.

The next step which I have to implement is to have cheap webcams detect movement and tell one of the machines with another 8 way relay - which I have yet to buy - which room I'm in and have it switch the speaker output to that room, and perhaps switch off the room I've just walked from.

I'd love to do face recognision with it too, so that it could randomly choose a song it knows I like when I walk in, or if my GF is with me, it could choose a song of her liking instead - this is half way there, as when you choose music through either the touchscreen box or the media centre, you can choose to tell it who is choosing songs - this way, I've built up a table of most commonly played songs and by whom. One of the options on the touch screen is "random song" - you can make it choose from the top 100 most common, or from the whole lot.

I can't imagine just how difficult this whole setup would have been if I had not discovered Ubuntu and still used Windows.

PS. I can post screenshots if anyone is interested.

---

### Post by choyak on 2009-11-01
Age 45 and have Ubuntu on 2 PCs and 1 laptop.  I am using less and less of what I call 'Bugdows' (windows) the Ubuntu can do virtually everything that I need.  The only thing I needed Bugdows a few days ago was to Frostwire.  I now have Frostwire here on Karmic Koala so no need for Bugdows.  I confess I have one PC with Fedora 11 on it.  IMHO Ubuntu hardware implementation is much better than Bugdows.  I have a Dell XPS laptop and the Ubuntu installation by default located everything, even the WIFI card, and the Blu-Ray writer.  I did a backup on a Blu-Ray rewritable, try to do that with Vista you need to buy a program costs 300 billion dollars.  The one I am using now is a dual monitor setup and that was actually easy to implement (I have nVidia, not ATI, that is propably the reason).  I just love Compiz Fusion effects they are so well put together and it hasn't crashed on me yet but I do not use the cube because the effect is weird to me with dual monitors.  I have one monitor just open with FarmVille!!! from Zynga and the other one for everything else.  It is cool I configured my network drive to just show up on the desktop when booted, I had to use Terminal and edit fstab for that, but I an getting more accustomed to Terminal and Ubuntu is way more configurable than Bugdows will ever be.  I also don't need to worry about adware/malware/viruses.  One other thing, I use Ubuntu (or Fedora) to do ALL of my online bill paying and banking for fear of some crackware malware.

### Post by wiebeest on 2009-11-06
I am currently 33 years of age, using in *Ubuntu* since *Gutsy Gibbon* (7.10).

Before that I always was a **Windows** user, though I had become fascinated with the **Mac OS X**, which I ever-since always tried to mimic under *windows* and once even hacked my system into **OSx86 Tiger**, which I got working, but it was too restricted (since every update of the os caused problems and the lack of SSE3 instructions in my CPU made a lot of apps have to use emulated parts what hogged it down too much).   

So I started to look for alternatives. My first encounter with *Linux* was a **Knoppix live CD**, which already astonished me, by sheer fact it could completely run from live cd and still run on reasonable speed. 

So I expressed my awe to a close friend of mine who is a professional IT-er and is specialized in Linux. He liked the fact my first introduction to Linux had pleased me that much and suggested me to also try Ubuntu.   

So I then tried the *Ubuntu Gutsy live cd*, and discovered I liked that even more than my Knoppix cd (not knowing then that what pleased me so much was the Gnome interface instead of the KDE on the *Knoppix live-cd*).

With this *Ubuntu* version I did my first real Ubuntu/Linux install, through that was still a dual boot with *Windows XP* still as my primarily os.   

And I was amazed by the resource lightness of it, no hassle with firewalls and resource hogging anti-virus programs, anti-spyware and such, the then quite new Compiz fusion hype and the fact I could almost totally adjust the desktop to my own preferences. 

Before that, under *Windows* I was already very much into theming with the use of *stardocks* **WindowBlinds** and **Objectdock**, but which all costed money, and then in Ubuntu I could do that all too and more, but now (legally) for free.  
 
And since it's follow up Hardy Heron (8.04) I have made the complete switch from Windows XP (which at the time had failed me crashing after some shady program installs). 

Love **Karmic Koala** and it is blazingly fast on my system! 

Since I have completely switched to Ubuntu as my os, and thus I use it for everything. In my case this being surfing the web (both *Opera* and *Firefox*), chat (Epiphiny), the occasional gaming under Wine (ePSXe and a few Win games like in particular *Jagged Alliance2*, *Call of Duty2* and *Baldur's Gate2*), *Open office*, *terminal server client* for my work at the home office. 

And I enjoy getting to know Linux and getting more and more familiar and even comfortable with using the terminal, which didn't scare me all too much, being from the DOS age before *Windows 95*... ;-) 

Under *Ubuntu* I always install *gnome-do* (love it!!), and to keep my Mac-fetish satisfied ;-) I always install the *Mac4lin icons* and the *GTK Leopard theme * ([http://www.eamon63.deviantart.com/art/GTK-Leopard-for-Linux-121260672](http://www.eamon63.deviantart.com/art/GTK-Leopard-for-Linux-121260672)). Recently I switched from *AWN* to *Cairo dock*,

**BTW:** Hope Global-menu get's ported to 9.10 soon and will eventually support all apps.

### Post by jdunn on 2009-11-07
39.  I use it mostly for web browsing/email, document editing (w/OOo apps), java programming (w/ Eclipse), C++ programming (w/Code::blocks), link to my graphing calculator (w/ TiLP), some games (Neverball and WoG).

My laptop dualboots Vista with Ubuntu 9.10 but I spend most of my time on Ubuntu.  I've been using linux since 2001...I started with Mandrake, then migrated to Suse, then to Kubuntu in 2005 and Ubuntu in 2007.

I always prefered *nix to any other OS.  I was using Unix back in the early 1990's when Lynx and Mosaic were the only web browsers.  I'm very much anti-Microsoft/anti-Apple.  Having said that, I'm no linux fanboy because I realize that M$ Windows (or MACs) is usually the right OS for the average computer user.  Most people will always dread the CL.

### Post by Old_Grey_Wolf on 2009-11-10
> **Bearded-flower said:**
> O.o wow influx of uber young users, i didnt even get a PC till i was 15.

I think it will become more common for young or very young people to be using computers as the price of computers drop compared to peoples incomes.

In the late 1970's or early 1980's, when I bought my first personal computer, it cost me about **15% of my yearly income** for the computer, peripherals, and software. I was about 30 years old. I justified buying it because I was an engineer, and could use it to further my education, thus career. I could only afford one computer.

Move time 30 years forward....

I recently bought a Lenovo tower computer (with software, etc.) for about **20% of my weekly income**. As a percentage of my yearly income, I could buy 40 computers a year for what I paid for my first one. I have computers in most of the rooms in my house. My children and grandchildren can find one to use easily. There is a computer in the kitchen, dinning room, living room, exercise room, study, my workshop, 3 of the 6 bedrooms, laptops floating around the other rooms in the house, and none in the 3 bathrooms (yet! :)).

I remember when my 5-year-old grandson was watching a cartoon channel on the TV. The channel was advertising an Internet game. My 5-year-old grandson when to the computer in the living room, and used Google to find the game.** I didn't know he could read!**.

As I suggested above, as the price of computers drop compared to peoples incomes we will see younger people having the **opportunity** to use a personal computer.

### Post by bgreenaway on 2009-11-16
53. After experimenting with many different distros, I have been using Ubuntu for two years or so now, mainly for web, music, email and 'playing' with Ubuntu. I also love the concept of open-source software and more power to it and the vast community that supports it. Having been in IT for 25+ years and worked in a Unix OS-based support and consultancy environment using many different variations, I have a lot of time for Ubuntu. 

I have also been an Ubuntu evangelist and rebuilt or repaired friends' desktops/laptops and replaced the ubiquitous Windows with Ubuntu and (nearly all) people love it. 

There is a serious side as well: I also use it at work in an Active Directory domain. The difficulty is persuading some people - the 'it is free and not Microsoft, so it must be crap' argument. Personally, I will never use Micro$oft OSes again.

### Post by brion@cbkidder.com on 2009-12-14
I am 48 years old, in Orange County California. I use Ubuntu on my laptop, which I use for work and for pleasure. I used to dual-boot with WinXP but now I only have Ubuntu on this machine.

The improvements that made it possible for me to switch over completely to Linux was the ability to run my company's Citrix virtual terminal and the ease of installing VMWare (which it turns out I haven't needed once since I installed it).

For work I do spreadsheets, documents, and desktop publishing (catalog & web). For pleasure I watch hulu or youtube, listen to my ipod via Rhythmbox, and play xmj mah-jong. Evolution email performs well and syncs to my Palm.

I wish Ubuntu had an interpreter for Windows drivers. For example, I just bought a SIIG USB video capture device, which comes only with Windows drivers, and no Linux drivers available. I can't use it unless I can figure out how to make the drivers work on my Lenovo laptop. Thus, a driver interpreter for Ubuntu would be an awesome feature.

Terminal used to infuriate me because I never want to have to do any line-level commands, ever. But I get over it each time my Linux system boots in under a minute, compared to the well over 5 minutes it took Windows.

### Post by Evie~ on 2010-01-10
I'm fifteen years old.

I became an Ubuntu user about three weeks ago by accident... I was only going to use it temporarily to backup all my files, and there were some serious start-up issues with XP. I tried to partition my only 40gb hard drive... Alas, it turns out I should really read up on how to do things, because I accidentally erased everything on there; music, movies, essays, programs (Photoshop!), pictures - **argh**!

So I threw myself into Linux with vengeance and tried my best to understand it all. So far I've done pretty well. I've worked out that pesky ipv6 thing in Firefox, how to install Flash & Java, simple commands to use in the terminal... The forums are heaven sent when I'm marooned in my little boat of inexperience amongst the high seas of endless questions ;)

All I use it for at the moment is general web browsing and playing an album off my USB stick, and I may switch back to Windows soon (just because I've grown up with it from the age of 7 and it's familiar :P). Sometimes I miss the simplicity of just reading a guide & installing some magical thing that fixes things for me, but I think I should step out of the box sometimes and learn something new. In the mean time, Ubuntu is great for doing simple things, and it's nicely laid out. Pleasure to use so far. :D

### Post by Riverside on 2010-02-12
I am 53 and have been using Linux full time since late 2002 (SuSE until April 2005, Ubuntu thereafter).  My desktop workstation is a Linux machine, I run the network abuse team for a UK ISP.  Most of the time it is essentially a dumb terminal as I work from a shell on a remote office server however I also edit and create some office documents using OpenOffice.  I also communicate with vocational (internal and external) contacts using AIM & MSN IM which I do using Pidgin (I haven't tried Empathy, Pidgin is absolutely perfect so I shall stick with it).

At home, I watch DVDs and TV using Ubuntu, surf the web primarily news and sport related sites including BBC iPlayer, play a few games occasionally (Mahjongg and BSD Cribbage being my favourites) and also access a shell account on a remote host where my main personal mail account is hosted (Mutt & Vim).

### Post by Dragonbite on 2010-02-16
Wow.. this thread has been going on for a while. Last time I posted, I was 37 and working with LTSP!

Now that I am 40 - 10 months, I am using Ubuntu as my primary operating system on my laptop and the family desktop as well as the server.

My laptop, though, I have multiple hard drives and trays so I swap them out to try new things, like openSUSE and Fedora.  For whatever reason, though, I find myself returning to Ubuntu.  It could be familiarity, it could the be the user-friendly tweaks or it could be that it just works!  

Regardless, I am trying to force myself to do something WITH the computer instead of constantly tinkering and trying new distros.  I *AM* my own worst enemy of maintaining a stable, consistant computer environment for the family!

The desktop is the primary workhorse of the family, although it is going to be getting swapped out for a more powerful system which currently has Windows 7 installed on it. My goal is to set it up with Ubuntu LTSs and go LTS to LTS.

Funny thing is the only person intersted in using Windows 7 at all (other than me) is my son and, as he states it, is only because it's "new".  We'll see how long that lasts when 10.04 is released.

My server runs Ubuntu Server 8.04 LTS and will probably be swapped out when 10.04 LTS comes out.  Unfortunately it has been the victim of my tinkering so I am figuring that by 10.04 I should have a better understanding of how things work, how i want them to work, and how to keep me from messing it up again!  Not to mention, when 10.04 is put on the more powerful system for the desktop, the current desktop may be re-appropriated into a server for me!

### Post by JCollierDavis on 2010-02-17
32 Years old.  Likely one of the oldest people to have had a computer my whole life.

I have lots of computers, but they break down into two real categories of use.
1. At work-  Windows Vista (Ick).  Mostly office applications, lots of spreadsheets, databases, etc. Works well for this if you remove the suck that is Vista.
2. At home- UNR.
 
My personal laptop is a Toshiba U200 circa 2008.  I run Netbook Remix and spent quite a bit of time customizing the menus etc, limited to sytle changes though.  UNR is great for me because in my personal life I don't really do lots of spreadsheeting, if you know what I mean.  I use it for getting on the internet, loading music on my phone, watching videos, but mostly communicating. 
UNR is clean, simple, easy to figure out and presents all the stuff I use daily where I want it and everything else "in the closet" I say.  I know where it's at the one time a month I need to use an applicaiton like "manage groups."

### Post by dprestemon on 2010-02-21
I'm 64 and Ubuntu is pretty central to my daily life.  I spend the work week away from home, staying in a small studio apartment.  My desktop running Karmic is my radio (Morning Edition for breakfast), my movie player and my music player.  I log into a Lotus Notes server to get messages and documents from work, and I get my personal e-mail. I do nearly all my shopping online, pay my bills, buy tickets, manage my bank account, etc.  I keep the family budget on an Open Office Spreadsheet, and write with Open Office word processor.  And of course I surf for news, sports and anything else that comes to mind.  At home, I have a laptop running Hardy, not because I don't prefer Karmic,but just because it was such a hassle to get Hardy to work with my machine that I'm leery of starting over with a new upgrade.  All in all, I doubt that I have a need to resort to Windows more than once or twice a year.

### Post by bpan123 on 2010-02-22
I'll be 49 in June, and have installed Ubuntu 9.10 on several of my friends computers now (they're neophytes)  I have it installed on my 2nd computer (an Intel D945GCLF), and would "like" to install it on my primary computer, but it doesn't support my DTV video card (Hauppauge WinTV-HVR-1250)

I don't have cable, I watch OTA (over-the-air) tv in HD using the Hauppauge card - and that is the ONLY real reason I won't change my primary PC from WinXP to Ubuntu...

Until such time (currently investigating SageTV...) I will continue to surf using Ubuntu.

My main PC (AMD Athlon 64 X2 (5200+) will remain WinXP until someone can convince me that the THIRD option can supports my full needs.

Don't get me wrong - I love Ubuntu - but it (and Linux) are still distant cousins as far as hardware support are concerned.  This is my main concern.

Ubuntu is a great effort - and when I begin to see major hardware manufacturers providing drivers for Windows AND Ubuntu compatible versions of Linux - THEN I will convert 100%.

From what I have seen, the time is near!  

(I am hopeful!!!)

### Post by Len Tyree on 2010-03-02
I am 77 tears old, (will be 78 this month), a retired Air Force Non Commissioned Officer, 20 years of service, a Vietnam and Korean vet.  Also a retired computer programmer, 30 years + of COBOL and other COBOL type languages, all on IBM, VAX and PC type machines.   
I use linux(ubuntu 9.10) for programming, and for e-mail, keeping in touch with fam & friends, etc, to keep up with the news (as the olympics in Vancouvr BC). C does not do enough for me, C++ is better, but Java, I think, will fit my desires/needs.  I do not believe in going from 1 program to another to accomplish a job. ### Post by g4ry.l33 on 2010-03-05

compilers like g++ are great! Easy to use and fun to learn with. And

time that's the first thing on my list. Just didn't do things like that
on Windus..  just point and click.

customize the OS? Just can't do that with Windus...

hey.. that's part of learning.

self as well.

### Post by kaldor on 2010-03-07

### Post by samalex on 2010-03-08
I think I posted to this thread a while back, but after reading some of the newer posts I had to put another comment out there.

For the kids who are like 16 or younger you're SO freakin' lucky to have this kind of technology to play with as a teen.  I've been into computers since I was around 11 or 12, which was in the mid 80's, and back then you were lucky if you had a computer with a floppy drive :)   Most of my free time as a teen through Jr High and High School was spent learning how to program in Basic and Pascal, tinkering with hardware (anyone remember PEEKs and POKEs, MFM Hard Drives, or CGA/EGA/Hercules monitors??), plus hanging out on BBSes.  If you mentioned 'computer' to anyone you got a stare since most didn't know what you were talking about.  And say nothing of Online, BBS, Email, Modem, etc.

My wife and I were talking last night about what kinda projects we would've tackled given technology back in was where it is now.  Being huge Clerks fans and seeing what Kevin Smith went through just to make Clerks, now'days given you have access to decent actors you can SO do something like that now with a MacBook, iMovie, and a nice DV Camera without maxing out your credit cards.  I mean heck I've seen fan flicks on Youtube that blow the special effects of movies even 10 years ago outta the water.

And yes it's weird thinking there are people in here that were born after I graduated high school, which doesn't seem all that long ago :-/

Just some thoughts---  

Sam

---

### Post by Dragonbite on 2010-03-08
> **samalex said:**
> I think I posted to this thread a while back, but after reading some of the newer posts I had to put another comment out there.

For the kids who are like 16 or younger you're SO freakin' lucky to have this kind of technology to play with as a teen.  I've been into computers since I was around 11 or 12, which was in the mid 80's, and back then you were lucky if you had a computer with a floppy drive :)   Most of my free time as a teen through Jr High and High School was spent learning how to program in Basic and Pascal, tinkering with hardware (anyone remember PEEKs and POKEs, MFM Hard Drives, or CGA/EGA/Hercules monitors??), plus hanging out on BBSes.  If you mentioned 'computer' to anyone you got a stare since most didn't know what you were talking about.  And say nothing of Online, BBS, Email, Modem, etc.

My wife and I were talking last night about what kinda projects we would've tackled given technology back in was where it is now.  Being huge Clerks fans and seeing what Kevin Smith went through just to make Clerks, now'days given you have access to decent actors you can SO do something like that now with a MacBook, iMovie, and a nice DV Camera without maxing out your credit cards.  I mean heck I've seen fan flicks on Youtube that blow the special effects of movies even 10 years ago outta the water.

And yes it's weird thinking there are people in here that were born after I graduated high school, which doesn't seem all that long ago :-/

Just some thoughts---  

Sam

Just gotta face it.. we're old. ;)

I took Basic and Pascal in High School but that was not as helpful as Typing was on the old monochrome IBM word processor.

Unfortunately a friend of mine may have had a computer, but I didn't get my hands on anything outside of college (Win 3.1 and Vax VT100 terminal) until when my Dad got his first PC running Windows 95.

After that.. everything changed!

---

### Post by samalex on 2010-03-08
> **Dragonbite said:**
> Just gotta face it.. we're old. ;)

I took Basic and Pascal in High School but that was not as helpful as Typing was on the old monochrome IBM word processor.

Unfortunately a friend of mine may have had a computer, but I didn't get my hands on anything outside of college (Win 3.1 and Vax VT100 terminal) until when my Dad got his first PC running Windows 95.

After that.. everything changed!

Yeah our typing classes were on Apple][ systems and yup the green phosphorous monitors.  Too many hours of Oregon Trail :)  At least by the time I got into 11th grade they had moved up to Mac's so that was nice.  The IBM lab still had PS2's when I graduated, but worked great to learn Lotus 123 and WordPerfect.

At any rate, at home I didn't get my first PC until 1990 when I got an 8088 clone running MS-DOS 3.3 (had various TRS-80 systems until then), and that plus Turbo Pascal and my modem made me a happy camper through high school.  It's funny, I remember one weekend me and a friend spent two full nights trying to make it through LSL and hitting FidoNet looking for clues on what to do next.  We eventually made it to the end, but never did make the total amount of points possible.  

It's funny... as I said before I wish we had today's technology when I was younger, yet I long for those days of surfing BBSes, poking around Tymnet and Telenet, and being a mischievous kid on the local echos.  Too bad there's no FidoNet archive like they have for Usenet going back to the 80's and 90's...

Yup, we're old indeed.. and I'm only 34 :-/  

Take care --

Sam

### Post by murderslastcrow on 2010-04-04
Gyaaaarrr, you young'nz n' your programmin'!

I'm 21, and I use Ubuntu for so many things it's hard to list them. I do tons of 3d modelling with Blender and Wine-enabled programs, texturing in GIMP, web design in GIMP and Kompozer, adding subtitles to foreign videos with Openshot (which is good at plenty of other tasks, so glad it's in Lucid's repositories!), Gedit and IDLE for Python programming, getting started with some test applications with BGE (Blender Game Engine), hoping I can port some to WebGL some time in the future...

I don't use Office software much at all, except for writing the occasional novel or focused-content article.

I love listening to my music in Ubuntu because of all the players and plugins available- you'll finding me switching between music players rapidly, as well as using a lot of web applications for music.

I spend a lot of time reading information on just about anything, and it's gotten even more addiction with the speed Google Chrome lets you browse. Broadband indeed.

I'm also starting to enjoy a lot of open source games, and I hope to contribute some more advanced titles as my knowledge of Blender increases. I love Ubuntu, love Debian, love open source, and Wine doesn't hurt it either XD. Best Operating System around, by far.

### Post by admiralspark on 2010-04-19
I'm 18, and I use Ubuntu as my primary OS.

-Glade3 GUI creation

-Torrenting
-Skyping
-teaching myself Wireshark on here because I'm too lazy to boot BackTrack off the USB
-Jokosher/PiTiVi editing of music/movies
and finally, most importantly: managing my other OS's through Unetbootin/CD's and using Ubuntu to troubleshoot when I kill them. Because when the other partition with XP dies on me, I have to boot to Ubuntu to save it (or spend 10 hours tracking them down since apparently I own an obscure pre-lenovo Thinkpad).

The possibilities are endless, and Ubuntu is the easiest way to learn anything *nix you'd ever want to know!

went back to winXP

### Post by jonazdnb on 2010-05-02
I'm 19, I use Ubuntu for server purposes, torrenting, LAMPserver, regular ftp and ssh, and much more. I've been using Ubuntu for a few weeks now, I used Arch before for about 4-5 years and hated everything about Ubuntu, being mainstream and bloaty and so on.
But, I've totally changed my mind since I tested the server version, much cleaner, and I can build it myself from the ground up with the packages and other stuff that I want. I still think regular *buntu is bloaty, but thats just good, theres something for everyone. :)
It's feels abit difficult to switch from Arch cause of the filesystem differences, but all in all both Arch and Ubuntu is Linux, so it shouldn't be a big problem. :)

So why did I switch?
The Ubuntu community is alot bigger, also with Arch being a cutting edge distro releasing new packages as they are available, complications often occur. I used to have time and had alot of fun fixing around in my system and tweaking and so on.
Unfortunately I don't have that time anymore and Ubuntu fits me perfectly. If I want a cutting edge release of something I can install it easy anyways. :)

Although i have Arch to thank for learning Linux, before Arch I tested everything out there, I never really got into the depth of Linux, Arch changed that totally.
But now I'm ready to move on again, Ubuntu is my future now. :P

---

### Post by Rakali on 2010-05-03
43... Was having more and more problems on this box with a certain expensive OS, and looking at an equally expensive upgrade. Athlon 2800, 1 gig DDR @333 yadda yadda some old vid card with 256 or something. I do love the AMD chips though. 

Years ago my de facto had a dual boot setup and network going. The memory of Linux was that it worked, but I didn't know how to get it that way. Seemed awfully technical. Constant system resets due to the Australian summer heat kind of pushed me over the edge and when the HDD died taking all my data with it, there was no reason not to try Ubuntu.

Surprise, surprise, it works! I'm still amazed every time I turn the thing on and it's all just there. No going off for a coffee and coming back to find it still loading or anything.

So I got a working OS and faster system, all for free. 

I use it for everything except Civ 2. One of these days I'm gonna do a serious search and headbutt the wall until it goes LOL but haven't reached desperation point quite yet.

### Post by lisati on 2010-05-22

### Post by SoFl W on 2010-05-22
When I started computing we had 1/2 a k of memory, we had to share it with all my brothers and sisters and WE LIKED IT.
My modem consisted of two tin cans with a piece of twine between the two.  It took four days to transfer a black and white picture of a girl with her stockings rolled down.  (that was porn back then) 
Our keyboard had only 25 keys on it because the letter Q hadn't been admitted to the alphabet yet.
It took three days to add up the pennies from selling newspapers on the corner but if we made over a dollar the computer would crash because it couldn't handle all those digits.

---

### Post by Tombgeek on 2010-06-17

I use Ubuntu for practically everything: music, Internet, videos, word processing (creative writing, essays, etc.). All my main tasks.

I have to use Windows XP for school, because I need to use Delphi 7 and Microsoft Office 2007 (Excel and Access) for IT. I am not allowed to use an alternative, and as far as I know, both programs do not work well under WINE.
So dual boot FTW!!!
.
.
But I still hate Windows.

### Post by Dragonbite on 2010-06-21

### Post by AverellTorrent on 2010-07-08

### Post by Fatman_UK on 2010-07-29
30. Everything except gaming. Which in my case means:
* idle browsing (chromium-browser)
* photo editing (gimp, imagemagick)
* emails (thunderbird)
* web server (apache)
* private SMTP relay (postfix)
* haven't really got POP or IMAP working yet, my relay is send-only atm
* backups (bacula)
* raytracing (povray)
* programming (codeblocks, gcc, valgrind)
* database operations (mysql or, latterly, postgres)
* monitoring (nagios)
* PXE boot server (atftpd)
* DNS (bind9)
* creating/reading documents (openoffice.org)
* network security auditing (nessus)
* VPN (openvpn)
* creating sound effects (audacity)
* remote access (ssh)
* instant messaging (psi)
* file transfer (filezilla, scp)
* project management (ganttproject)
* secret management (keepassx)
* music (lastfm)
* network troubleshooting (a whole bunch of tools)
* ISO burning (brasero)
* IRC (xchat2)
* creating diagrams (dia, qcad)
* torrents (rtorrent) - Linux or BSD ISOs only of course ;) 
* packet sniffing (wireshark)

and other stuff too, but the list is getting too long.

### Post by urinophoria on 2010-08-09
33 and *almost* everything.  Remote administration, web browsing, network troubleshooting, security analysis, GigE industrial vision troubleshooting, email, and sudoku. Unbelievable how effortlessly an inspiron mini takes to networking tasks with minimal (if any) modification to ubuntu.  All of the tools I use are in the ubuntu software center or synaptic package, with default repositories. Well, aside from the GigE cameras, but that's ok, I don't mind doing a little of the work myself.  :)

Also great for home entertainment; an old computer, wireless keyboard, mouse and a basic ubuntu installation make a pretty satisfactory dvd/avi/mp3/cd player, throw in an FM tuner (or Pandora) and it's a great all in one entertainment center. Get just enough graphics card for DVI out, and a decent sound card, and that old hangar queen on your bottom shelf goes back to being a daily use PC.

The only thing I don't use Ubuntu for is Aion, Still haven't been able to get it to run without some form of Micro$hite license, so my biggest and best computer is a dual-boot.  Everything else gets formatted and Ubuntu'd as soon as I get it out of the box.

### Post by robbiemacg on 2010-08-14
I'm 29.
I run Ubuntu on a notebook (Lenovo Y550) that I use for business/travel.
I run a publishing firm, producing print editions and electronic texts (EPUBs). I use Ubuntu to do all the same things I use the desktop G5 in my office for: email, social media, managing files and data (locally and in the cloud), preparing documents, and proofing projects. I also use Ubuntu to develop and deliver presentations (as it's running on my portable machine).
Though I started using Ubuntu to save money, I can't get enough. I love being able to customize my experience, I love that sophisticated dev tools are available to everyone, I love the community support. I'm a very happy user.

*Side note: I knew nothing about how the tools I used everyday worked until I started using Ubuntu (about 6 months back). I now understand the basic principals of computing! I have committed many Bash commands to memory, written my own scripts, and learned a good bit of Python.

### Post by Windwalker52 on 2010-08-20
I'm 53, I started at 17 in the Navy with hardwired electronics and I have run a DOS based BBS with a 3.1 sysop shell after a love affair with a Tandy TL/2 using Radio Shack proprietary software. I graduated to XP and found it crashing due to my experiments too often. I tried Ubuntu 9.04 and finally crashed it after 500+ programs installed. I came back after a blue screen of death from XP and a warning by Microsoft that I had used the COA too often to reinstall XP. I love to learn. That sounds freaky to some people but I am not trying for normal.
 I am currently running 10.04 with a XP dual boot for Freelancer Discovery so I can learn how to create my own universe and then go play in it.
 I only have one working machine which is a dual core 2.5GHz with 4GB of 800MHz Ram and 500 GB of space internally using 10.04 until 10.10 comes out.
 I do everything I can think of on the computer. I write in Open Office, surf (of course), gaming ( I am currently trying to install X-Plane) possibly another BBS system (telnet),
and everything else that occupies a good part of my day. I don't own or have in my possession a television and I don't own a radio beyond the computer and the net.
 I started with Ubuntu about 2 years ago with a rebuild after every XP crash. A friend (sort of) introduced me to Linux as I was trying to find my focus in computer sciences and possibly start a firm. I plan to do anything I want to keep on Linux and Ubuntu. I have been focused in this direction for less than a year now and I am slowly learning terminal commands. 
 I would like to get some newbie reading material for adults on Linux even if it is not free and I am still trying to find a downloadable copy of the Ubuntu manual.
 It is great to hear so many voices and such a diverse group of people. Later.
):P

### Post by libssd on 2010-08-20
When I turned 60, I decided to ride a motorcycle to Alaska. I had six weeks to ponder what I was doing with my life, and decided to get out of the field of libraries and computers, which had occupied my working life since 1980. No regrets.

I cut my teeth on an IBM model 29 card punch at UCLA in 1970, and while watching the 8-year old at the output desk waiting for his program to extract square roots to finish, I realized that I was never going to be a programmer.

[IMG]http://www-03.ibm.com/ibm/history/exhibits/vintage/images/4506VV4002.jpg[/IMG]
*That's not a computer -- it's merely an input device. The computer was **much** bigger.*

I started playing around with Ubuntu in May 2009 to learn more about *nix, at a deeper level than in my working days, and I hoped that the challenges of solving problems would help keep my mind sharp. I am a guinea pig in a longitudinal health study, and I'm pleased to say that after 15 months of mucking around with Linux, my cognitive function test scores this month were the highest in the two years that I have been a participant. How's that for a testimonial from a different direction?

---

### Post by qazwiz on 2010-08-26
53 last month... I use Linux for everything... and currently that means Ubuntu

I had a Micro$hit machine that i installed as many Open Source programs ported to Windoze that i could find originally this was just OpenOffice and GIMP... i kept having to re-image my drive so i eventually started duel booting with various linux versions. when my computer gasped its last I was given a Virus locked Dell machine (don't ever buy a machine, no matter what OS, that requires the fall-back boot be a different partition on the same drive.... viruses can access them too easily.  my friend could have retained this computer if he had possession of a dvd to re-image the drive

so I used a livecd of hacker tools to reformat the drive, no more virus... but also no OS.  then used a livecd to install a Linux OS (either Ubuntu 8 or OpenSuse of same time frame) and have been running Linux ever since... had about a dozen different flavors on this machine including puppy for a while but have gravitated to Suse and Ubuntu for their ease of install and upgrade... Fedora has never been installed despite my trying a number of times... either no image available to DL and install or the install doesnt recognize something i have connected - - - you almost must have a keyboard that works!

and from what i hear, Redhat RPM files don't install dependencies you need to look for them..... much better to use Ubuntu which I'm gravitating towards since Suse Enterprise doesn't have p2p transfers... bandwidth was a killer there

---

### Post by qazwiz on 2010-08-26
> **waynepd said:**
> ..... I am hoping Ubuntu Studio will meet my needs otherwise i will be back to Cubase on Windoze. :(

before you go back be sure you tried a system that had a core optimized for audio/video recording/editing... I assume that Studio does but am not positive

the general core used by most is a "lock,  stock and barrel" setup that slows down operations  to allow you to do most anything that you would want to do.... this slows the system but isn't noticeable to most.... real time events (RTE) on the other hand, can noticeably be slowed even to video that is unusable for some slower machines


the two fixes are a faster machine and a better optimized OS that speeds up RTE processing at the expence of other things that hopefully you wont want to use

a faster machine usually means buy more expensive hardware including: video card, audio card, Memory and last but first, a FASTER CPU....  and this is a very $$$ answer

an Optimized Operating system that allows priority for RTE will allow real Time Capture of sound and video on a machine near the "bare bones" speed... this can be further aided by removing any unnecessary  programs and not automatically starting those things you don't need while Capture is in progress

a dedicated machine can turn off Internet access and anti-virus checking during the capture process to make better raw capture.... but if the machine is ever used to connect to the internet the anti-virus needs to be restarted immediatly after capture is finished

I am positive that you will be able to find an Open Source solution

### Post by 0000_soldier on 2010-10-22

### Post by t.rei on 2010-10-29
32. I use ubuntu - that is the gnome desktop - because of all the Userinterfaces this is the one I got to where is actually helps me getting things done my way instead of making me do things the way someone else thought them to be standard. And the amount of hardware supported by ubuntu/linux is just awesome and keeps me away from "google the drivers".

Been around many operating systems. Probably also due to working with them. And I shamelessly rip of elements that I like from OSX, Windows 3.11 - 7 ;) I also combine the elements of the UI to my liking. 

And I kindof enjoy the way working gets so much enjoyable if the friction of other-peoples-standard-ideas need not apply.

- The usual Office and Web Work (Text, Presentations, Email, Chat, Browsing)
- The usual Home Entertainment (Music, Movies, DvDs, Web)
- Gaming (unfortunately my time is too sparse lately: eve-online,WoW,...)
- Work (Programming: Smalltalk, C++, C, Java,...; Translations, Papers)
- Personal Computer-Based Hobbies (will try to make all those fictions and fantasies of mine into a book probably)

### Post by pungwe on 2010-12-22
> Anyone have any ideas how to sort OpenOffice I just find the formatting is never the same, not necessarily the font etc but more the spacing and layout. Can write up a cracking bit of work in op take it into college, open in word and it all cocks up.


Make sure that you have the original fonts installed.

I find that even on *Windows* if the font that the original was sent in is not on the local machine it will screw up. by the way *Microsoft* changed the  MSWord default font from Arial to Calabari. Unfortunately Open Office does not have a way to embed fonts.

I find that if I specify Arial word seems to use the correct format.

P.S. If the person receiving the file does not need to edit it then export it as a PDF from OpenOfice and adobe reader will not muck about with the formatting.

### Post by duf on 2011-01-02
You stop at the age of 45. What about older people?

I am 69 (well in just ten days) and I worked on computers when most of you were not yet born.
I started with a Honeywell-Bull (punch cards) to program in assembler, After a while I moved to another company which had a MAI programming in Buissiness Basic (we managed a database of over 30000 items)
My first PC was a DOS 1.3 machine with two single sided 5.1/4 floppies and 128 KB ram and we did have a wordprocessor, spreadsheet, database, reportbuilder.
I started it up a week ago and it was still working. I threw it away and I am already sorry for that. Try to find today a programmer who can make a database or spreadsheet or wordprocessor which fit in 68KB of memory.

I now use linux on a X86_64 for my buisiness, or to program for friends most in scripting languages, occassionnally in C (I dont'like C++).

### Post by miekrand on 2011-01-12
I am 28 years old and I use Ubuntu for researching, studying, programing, web browsing, entertainment and every day desktop use.  I have been exclusively using Ubuntu since about mid December.  There are no other Operating Systems installed to my hard drive.  I am not new to Linux Operating Systems in the sense of I have installed and played with many Linux Flavors several years ago.  However after getting tired of Windows and being Inspired by my best friend whom I worked in Retail Sales with for a few years.  He Inspired me to install Ubuntu after all these years.  This particular friend of mine is so impressive, he went from being homeless to being a manager of an art store, gradually of course.  He always researches anything he buys and anything he puts in his computer.  I trust his opinions and he has never steered me wrong.

Why do I like Ubuntu so much that almost one month later I am loving it?

It is easy to use and has fantastic support for its users.
It is documented, extremely well, thats important because I actualy read those manuals.
It is highly customizable, I can change anything I want.
Comes with TAR that works, I can use it for a complete backup too.
I own my Ram, don't have to worry about strange programs hogging it.
It does what I tell it, No worrying about whats hogging my CPU?
Gives users a nice cloud to upload files to, thats something I can see myself paying for.
It is built around a kernel that works, and a file system that is standardized.

No more Anti-virus necessary, I still have to be careful what scripts I run but its easy to read the source.  Reading a .exe is not as easy.

A predictable regular release schedule, I can plan my work around this.

Comes with open source software, I can enter commercial development without spending thousands on expensive CAD software, SPICE Simulators or ASIC design software.  Does not mean everything has to be free but I dont have 5000$ in my pockets for software.

It Comes with a user friendly Software Update system and Software Center, but does not limit its users to those programs.  Synaptic is available along with apt and its suit of tools.  I have a choice over how I manage my software and can switch back and fourth at will.

Nice theme options available at gnome-look and gnome-art along with many others, although not Ubuntu specific Mira v2 is the best , 'hint'

In fact I cannot stop thinking of reasons I use Ubuntu, for sake of keeping it short we will terminate this list here, there might be a buffer limit.

---

### Post by celem on 2011-03-01
Why would your poll stop at 45? I am 64 and Linux is my ONLY OS. I abandoned Windows once Linux reached sufficient desktop functionality that I could toss Microsoft out.

I have used Unix since 1974 when I had a login on a Dec PDP11/30. Since I worked for AT&T, I had access to Unix source code and used to hack around with it quite a bit in the 1980s.

Think about your age brackets on the poll. Unix has existed since 1970 and people, like me, that cut their teeth on Unix were thrilled when Linux came around. I booted my first Linux system in the 1990s from a pile of floppys. Folks like me would have been in their early 20s when Unix was created. Therefore, your age brackets should have gone up into the 60s.

By the way, I use Mint-9/Ubuntu 10.04 on my desktop for all general home computing and on a Dell mini 910 for a travel system.

### Post by sixstorm on 2011-03-05
I'm sure I've posted in here before but I'll give an update.

I've finally had the chance to purchase the laptop of my dreams:  Macbook Pro.  So I'm currently a Mac guy with quite a few VMs such as Windows 7 Pro, Arch Linux and Ubuntu 10.10.  I have the choice of any OS I want to run and it all blends perfectly.  Also the wife will be getting a MBP soon.  I do have a nice Windows 7 gaming rig but I'm addicted to gaming.  ;)

I'm currently an IT support specialists who does some work on the side, so when I see people with 3+ year old PCs with Vista or XP on them (and when it's pretty screwed up from viruses and whatnot), I won't reinstall Windows but will rather install Ubuntu.  One of my family members has my 6 years old custom PC with WinXP on it and they trashed it with some virus.  Instead of offering to put XP back on it, I put Ubuntu on it, they already used Firefox and made sure Skype would work with their webcam.  Everything worked perfectly out of the box and ran a whole lot faster.

I still love to tinker with Linux and love to hear about how it's progressing in the community, I think it's the best community project ever.

### Post by Mr Tickle on 2011-03-05
I'm 19 currently studying an undergraduate Masters in Computer Science (1st year atm) at Loughborough University. 
I have been tinkering around with Ubuntu and Linux in general for around 2-3 years, I found it to be of an advantage in understanding its Unix like foundation (trial and error) when it came to many of my modules at university, so I am glad I came across it and stuck with it :) 

I love the fact that my whole ubuntu system is on my portable hdd (along with other linux distros) so I can take it anywhere and just boot from usb whilst getting fairly decent read/write speeds.

I use Ubuntu for programming in Java and Haskell at the moment. The thing I really love about Ubuntu and Linux in general is its efficiency (really snappy) and the 'no fear' of viruses when I browse web sites. 

All in all a solid distro, a fantastic community and a wonderful user experience.

### Post by Smallwheels on 2011-03-27
For now I mostly use my 2008 Mac Book. I started my Linux adventure last June when Vista (the crappiest OS ever created) got a virus. My dual boot Vista/Ubuntu was having problems all the time and I would work on them one at a time every few weeks. Just this week I got all the browsers working properly (for the most part). 

I've got my HP printer working, DVD playback working, Skype working, e-mail working, and I just signed up for Dropbox and love it. Finally something that works the first time without tweaking. 

My intention is to eventually use Ubuntu for everything. At this point it won't operate some of my programs that only work on Windows. Perhaps if I learn to use Wine I can stay in Ubuntu. 

My computers are used for streaming media, writing, creating presentations, editing movies, telephone calls, keeping memories. If Ubuntu would work with Silverlight I could use it to watch the Starz channel on Netflix. 

Windows XP chased me away from Windows to Leopard. Leopard is great. Really great. Ubuntu is just as fast even though it is running on much slower hardware. 

I'm 48 years old and eventually Ubuntu will be my main computer once it is set up to do everything I need done. I don't know if that will ever happen. I'm just glad that I won't need to buy another Apple computer in order to have a good operating system.

### Post by Sparky69 on 2011-04-12
26, I'm pretty new to Linux/Ubuntu, took a bit of getting used to but I think I'm in love=D>!!
Pretty much the the same a window$, I wanted to dual boot window$ to keep one partition clean (windows with all the software I used on it took up about 150G, pain in the **** when you mess something up and have to reinstall) and another to try thinks out with but window$ is quite hefty from vista upwards so I stumbled upon Linux!! Plus I want to see how much better Linux handles graphic design on my 1.2 GHz processor.

Ps- A little tip if you find you can't get the occasional piece of software working under Ubuntu (& haven't got your head round wine yet](*,)) Get a copy of xp, get a copy of nlite ([http://www.nliteos.com/nlite.html](http://www.nliteos.com/nlite.html)) and trim it down. Xp only takes up 2.6G and most things are still compatible with it. Load xp into virtual box ([http://www.virtualbox.org/](http://www.virtualbox.org/)) and trim down window$ as small as you can while still being able to run what you want to run , It will take a few tries. And if you really can't stand windows there's always Puppy Linux (apparently you can load it inside windows with Qemu the same as Damn Small Linux but I haven't tried it yet).

### Post by BertN45 on 2011-04-19
Almost 66, During previous year I used it for work, if I worked at home. In the office (EUROCONTROL) they used Microsoft. It reduced the size and often the colleagues could use those re-converted files again in Word. So Open Office to save MS-Word. I used it to access my file-server in Santiago from Europe.

Now I use Skype and Evolution to stay in contact with my brothers, sons and grand children in between my visits to Europe. I use it to read new papers; the New York Times and Dutch Papers. I see a couple of times per week the Dutch TV mainly the news. I use it to learn more Spanish.

I use it also to organize all my digital belongings, music collection, photos, video etc. I had to convert everything, because I moved to the other side of the Ocean and an USB stick is very easy to transport. 

I am installing a virtual museum of Operating Systems using VirtualBox. I experience again, what the 1 MB ram memory boundary meant in practice.

I promote Ubuntu. Step by step I convert the larger family here to Ubuntu. Away from the cracked windows versions, often supplied as enabler for other crimes.

### Post by Andavane on 2011-04-26

I live with two guys who had Windows.

### Post by aphatak on 2011-05-03
I am a crusty old lizard of 61, and I use Ubuntu for -
[LIST=1]
[*]Managing my audio library - recording live audio, editing, searching and playing

[*]Managing my video library - recording, editing, searching and playing

[*]Office productivity - word processing, spreadsheets, presentations, calendar- and task list

### Post by majorpay on 2011-05-03
I'm 34 and use the Server version for proving how hardcore a die-hard Windows user can be... :p
 
Actually, I have finally found some love for the shell and seen a Linux Server do some things I'm not even sure a Windows Server can do.  This has convinced me that I should always be looking over the other side of the fence.
 
I'd like to use it as my streaming media center and file sharing center as well.  The latter, I'm already doing, but the former does not seem to have any solid competitive offerings.  I can't remember the name of the one I ran in Windows (because I overwrote the installation with Ubuntu Server), but it had per device encoding, per device file permissions, supported all file formats I threw at it, ran really well (minus the fact that the transcoding for my DTV DVR sucked), and I believe it was only $25 (I only had the time-limited demo).

### Post by athenroy on 2011-05-17
61 and I find it sad that the cut off age of the poll was 45! :(  I build websites, write a music review blog, [Gulf Coast Music Review]("http://gcmusic.tmbler.net") and stream video on [Justin.TV]("http://www.justin.tv/crnkfm") before that it was internet radio.

I built a PC in the late 1990s which ran Win 98, 2 free versions of Red Hat Linux and IBM OS/2 Warp, all on one hard drive! :)

I've tried several earlier versions of Ubuntu from live CDs, but 11.04 is the first one that really seemed to work right on my current system and have the features I want and need.  I plan on switching as much of my activities as I can to Ubuntu.

I have an AS Degree in electronics from 1969 when we were amazed to see a basic 4 function calculator developed by HP using neon "nixie" tubes for read out!  PCs were a dream back than!  Real SciFi visions!  So I'm self-taught on computers.

You are never too old to learn! :)

### Post by oldsoundguy on 2011-07-15
> **KenGuev said:**

I started TRYING to use command line in Mandrake 4 (not much help from their forums) then went to Red Hat/Fedora with their REALLY snooty forums.  Since I began my computing in the DOS days BEFORE Windows, it was not that much of a stretch .. but a PITA and I needed help.

### Post by bern3485 on 2011-07-24

I have been using computers for over 35 years, mainframes, desktop PC's and Laptops.  I have decided to switch to a Linux Distro as the communities give quicker solutions to problems than do the support staff of propriety products.  I have just finished a seven week wait for a solution to a fairly simple problem with another O/S and the problem got worse, much worse before it was fixed.

### Post by Old_Grey_Wolf on 2011-07-24
> **bern3485 said:**
> :-D Hi, I am an old and decrepit 73 year old trying to learn a new O/S.  I will eventually use ubuntu for all my computer use but at present it is for surfing and email.  I have been using computers for over 35 years, mainframes, desktop PC's and Laptops.  I have decided to switch to a Linux Distro as the communities give quicker solutions to problems than do the support staff of propriety products.  I have just finished a seven week wait for a solution to a fairly simple problem with another O/S and the problem got worse, much worse before it was fixed.

I see you joined the Ubuntu Forum today. You are 10 years older than I am. You have used computers for about the same number of years as I have. Linux was easy for me to get accustomed to and I suppose it was for you as well. I have enjoyed it. I stay with the Long Term Support (LTS) releases, do backups, and a clean installs at each LTS release. Then I copy whatever I need from the /home folder from the backup to the fresh install. The LTS releases are in April of even numbered years and get security patches for 3 years. 8.04, 10.04 were LTS and 12.04 will be an LTS as well. The other non-LTS releases are supported for 18 months.

### Post by jblangi on 2011-09-25
I'm 69 and use Ubuntu for the challenge of learning, believe in open source, feed-up with Windows, it seems so crash proof, and if I screw it up I can just start over again with a new install. 

It does just about everything I need. So much is happening in the cloud - storage, docs, music, pictures, networking, email, news sources, e-learning, finance, video, e-books, e-shopping, and sharing just about anything. The OS is just a vehicle to access all of this and should not get in the way.  Ubuntu keeps it simple, inexpensive, and maximizes my hardware. And I am now waiting an Ubuntu tablet version to become readily available.

I find the YouTube demonstrations extremely helpful and hope more demo are added to the collection.  I was relieved to learn that we could use Gnome in 11.10.  I find Unity frustrating in managing Ubuntu.  My thanks to everyone who makes Ubuntu possible.

### Post by BenB1 on 2011-11-14
36-45 Group here.

I use the computer to communicate. It is nice to email friends and family, to be able to meet new people.

I use the computer to learn. There are lots of freely available resources on the Web and Internet which I would/could not access otherwise.

I use the computer to write for entertainment and information. Everyone likes a good story and with freedom to access educational resources, one may weave very fascinating tales.

I use the computer for spiritual input. Being able to communicate with others I find a greater sense of wholeness. Not an overly religious person here, even refuse being called an atheist because doing so feels religious to me. If someone presses for a definition, I usually respond with "a very vague and general Tao, man ... might be a Buddhist even, or a Zen monk ..." People are normally smart enough to not press further. I am a skeptic following the light.

I use the computer to be entertained. There are lots of fun games out there. I enjoy various genre of games, though find disillusionment in first person shooters. Served in the military but now feel I am better than taking the easy path, this carries over into gaming. As a result shooters don't quite hold me enthrall, nor do quest type of games which require one to 'grind' or 'farm' pointlessly. "Real life has that man. I play games to escape and relax, not repeat real life."

And I have used the computer to court lovers. Wound up marrying one, too. She's a great Lady. We will have been married for 12 years in April of 2012. We have been together for 14 years. Also courted another lover but that's a different tale for another time and place. Sorry if I prefer a little modesty and privacy.

I use the computer to create, to change, to live. Notice I say use the computer. I have used Windows, Linux both. I have used Debian and Ubuntu Linux. I am the type of person that really sees no point to differentiation, a computer is the tool used, the rest is merely preference trappings. I prefer Linux now, after growing frustrated at Windows' atrocities. "You mean I can't fix a program to work and not crash, on MY computer?" 

"Oh look, you can do that in Linux." *chuckle* I literally ran to Linux about six years ago. Windows had given me the last BSOD, and I had lost all data. It had taken about five years to get Windows up to preferences. Think I have only ever spent an hour total in configuring Linux stuff. And barring loose nut errors, data is safe nearly forever.

I also tried Red Hat many moons ago. Still like to look at different flavors. Use Fedora sometimes. Linux is Linux, though. Most of it is all pretty standard despite the push of being decentralized. That isn't a bad thing either in Linux's case.

And with that I'll close. Need to attend living.:)

---

### Post by what_indeed on 2011-12-02
I'm 38, I use ubuntu for everything including, but not limited to: Internet surfing, website design, graphics design, audio & visual editing/creation, listening to radio, study, research, writing, banking, bookkeeping, etc, etc, etc.

Haven't used Winderz in over a year except to fix or clear out my wife's computer.

I have a funny story about another ubuntu user.

My mom is 66 years old.  Recently she learned how to use a computer for the first time in her life.  I had the privilege of giving her her first computer and setting her up.  It, of course, had ubuntu.  So I trained her on how to surf the web, email and write in LibreOffice.

Well, her & my step-dad have been divorced for years but are still friends.  Even though their friends, he is still a cocky son-of-a-gun.  Acouple of years ago he got and learned to use his first computer.  Well, he has been bragging about all this stuff he has learned to do, add programs 'n stuff.  But he uses Windows XP.  He's been telling my mom that she is "old" (even though he is a year older than her!) because she doesn't know how to use a computer.

So when I gave my mom her computer and started showing her, I told her not to say anything to him for a few weeks and to hide it (it's a laptop) when he comes over to her place.  Well she has been picking it up quite well...

A couple of weeks ago my ex-step-dad said he put an administrator password on his laptop because his nephew would come over and surf to websites of ill repute when he wasn't home.  So my mom finally told him she got a computer.  Well, he started to kind of belittle her (he's not malicious, just a jerk sometimes) about how she had "much to learn" and so forth.  I just happened to be there when she was on the phone with him and I heard all this. So I whispered to my mom to tell him that Windows was for children and that she used a "true secure industrial-grade operating system" and that she could "hack" his admin password inside five minutes.

Keep in mind, she had no idea what she was saying, she was just repeating what I told her.  So he got all riled up and I told her to have him bring his laptop to her house so she could prove it.  Of course, he bit.  So I downloaded a WinXP password cracker, placed it on a flash drive and showed her how to use it (I used my wife's computer because it has WinXP).  She did it a couple of times, so the fun was ready happen.

So I left my bloggie rolling at her house on the day he came over and got it all on video...

The first thing he did was put his computer on the table and say "Ok, let's see you crack my password."  My mom is a champ, she said "I said hack, not crack, stand aside."  She inserted the flash drive, fired up his computer and just as she learned she hacked his admin password and got right in.  You should have seen the look on his face!  Ha ha.  Man, he was all bent out of shape and almost yelling "What is that, what is that?"  My mom played it real cool.  She said "It's part of my computer's ability." (Yeah, I told her to say that.)

Then she said, "It's pretty easy to use, here see."  She fired up her laptop and he went to lookin all close at it.  He said "What is this?"  She said "It's Linux."  He said what's that?  And she pointed at the desktop wallpaper that says "Linux- the Future of Technology"

You should have seen his face, his jaw was hanging two feet below his face.  The best part was when he said "Where's the start button?"

He ha, that was great.  My mom sat down and started to surf the internet and check her email quickly and flawlessly.  She said "Well, I'm sure this is all easy for you since you know computers so well and everything."  There's more but I don't want to bore you with the details.  But let's just say he's been quiet about his computer ability lately.

So I guess you could say I use ubuntu to have fun too.

Here is a screenshot of her her desktop:

### Post by rima on 2011-12-25
I'm 13. (seriously, I am). I use Ubuntu (mainly) for web browsing, graphics, word processing,and occasionally emailing. I never play any computer games, so really, I didn't lose anything at all~  Nobody, not even my parents, ever told me how to use Linux, none of my friends or family even told me Ubuntu existed (probobaly because they themselves didn't even hear much of it). Had to partition the drive myself, set up Firefox myself, download and install appropriate programs, set up firewall myself etc. I am alone in my Linux experience (though I'm not complaining, no one around me is experienced in computers, barely anyone even knows what a firewall is, so if they screwed my computer up...)  Many of my friends complained of crashed computers, full of adware and viruses. Although I myself almost never had a virus during my Windows experience (98 to ME to XP, then to Vista and again to XP) I decided to switch to Ubuntu for functionality.   I'm glad I switched to Ubuntu, eventhou' it's currently on dual boot with Windows, I rarely even use XP at all.

### Post by BertN45 on 2011-12-25
I'm 66. Just have fun and use virtualbox to look at all kind of flavours of OSes, including the old ones like Windows 1, 2 and 3, GEM, DRDOS, Ubuntu 4.10 etc. and the new ones like Ubuntu 12.04 and openSUSE 12.1. 

I use it for music, movie and, photos. Moving from Europe to the Dominican Republic I put almost all of my photos, vinyl, CDs and DVDs on the hard drive. I do not prefer the standard programs, but use Quod Libet or sometimes Clementine for music and Picasa 3.0 Beta for photos.

I use it for email, writing stuff, keeping track of the money, Skype, browsing. It gives me the opportunity to see most of the Dutch TV programs, if I want it. Still disappointed about the quality of silverlight/moonlight, so still using the windows media player streams. I use Ubuntu One to sync my documents folder.

I use Windows XP through virtualbox, but only for programs like Google sketch-up, format factory, my old outlook emails (2000-2009), the software for a photo film/dia scanner and the Samsung phone software (non android).

Plans are to get CPM running in Virtualbox, use Wine for Picasa 3.9 and SketchUp, look how to sync contacts from Google Mail to my Samsung GT-S5260, sync Gmail with Thunderbird and Evolution works.
