### Post by Welly Wu on 2012-02-07
I am 34 years old and I use Ubuntu for these reasons:

Rip and encode my CDs to .FLAC loss less audio files using RipperX and Sound Juicer
Play music using Clementine
Rip and copy DVD-Videos using K9copy
Watch DVD-Video movies using Fluendo DVD-Video player
Chat in real-time using Skype, X-IRC, Empathy
Read news using Google Chrome and Gwibber
Surf the Internet
Read and write e-mail messages using Mozilla Thunderbird
Encrypt e-mail messages using Enigmail and GPG
Store my user IDs and passwords using LastPass Pocket and Keepass 2
Read Amazon Kindle e-books using Amazon Cloud Reader
Read e-books using Calibre
Read digital magazines and Adobe .PDF files
Write documents using LibreOffice Writer
Encrypt data using TrueCrypt or GnuPG
Watch videos using VLC
Configure my firewall using GUFW
Use the BASH terminal to run commands
Install and remove software packages using Ubuntu Software Center
Synchronize encrypted data using DropBox and Ubuntu One
Study and do research on the Internet
Watch YouTube videos using Google Chrome
Backup my encrypted data using Deja Dup and GPG
Print documents using my Ironkey
File my federal and NJ state income taxes using TurboTax on the Internet
Secure and harden my Ubuntu 64 bit operating system
Upgrade to the latest Ubuntu 64 bit releases

I love Ubuntu 64 bit! It saves me an enormous amount of time and money compared to using Microsoft Windows 7 Ultimate 64 bit in which I had to pay for all of my software applications.

### Post by daslinkard on 2012-03-09
I'm 30...I use Ubuntu as my main OS.  I grew tired of the issues created continuously by Windows and decided to dive into Linux.  I started out with Mint Linux and then went to Ubuntu 11.10.  Currently I am trying to learn as much as I can as one day I would like to be a "Power User" in perspective to Linux.

I have aspirations of starting up a Linux Meetup group in my area as the closest group to me now is over 100 miles away.  I believe there are users in my area out there it just comes down to finding them.

As for what I use it for...I run my business off Linux as well as teach my children how to use the OS.  My 6 year old can navigate turning the PC on, going to PBS to play her games and we have started working on her usage of the Command Line Interface.  With that said we have been using the Tux Typing to help her learn the keyboard and knowing how to do the commands has actually helped her spelling and reading comprehension.

One day I would like to be able to look at my little girl and know that the successes she has in the world of Linux (preferably she gets a Linux admin job) is because of the grooming she started receiving at the young age of 6.  Who knows?

### Post by ExSuSEusr on 2012-06-14
I don't know about the rest of you, but I have noticed that with my age group - late 30's - people are either complete technophobes or tech-junkies.  Rarely do I meet someone in between.

For example - one of my friends has a computer that hardly gets turned on.  Another (same age range) has three systems, two tablets, who knows how many cell phones... 

It's strange.

Anyway, I use Ubuntu / Mint for everything.  Gaming, photography (finishing up my work - Gimp / Photoshop), media, work (real life work), education (self-teaching Perl and Java, learning languages), entertainment, managing business....  and a lot of other things... hell even social life to some extent.

The only think I don't keep on my systems (Linux or not) are high security information like bank accounts, credit cards, etc.... I am just too paranoid.  Good ole pen and paper with a lock box.

### Post by Brimwylf on 2012-06-16
I'm 29 and have been using Linux since the Redhat 5.2 days, but back on Ubuntu for about 2 months now.
The reason for using it is not really clear. I just...do. I feel really clumsy on any system without it (Linux, and Ubuntu in particular), because I'm used to do every little bit of my daily work on it, from browsing to file management. I sit inside Emacs and Chrome all day long, and the only PC game that I play is nethack. This coupled with the awesome underlying Linux structure and application suite makes a great home for my daily routine. Linux (and especially Ubuntu) just feels like home.
- I use it for Transmission. I'm loving the web interface (I run a secondary headless Ubuntu slave).
- I stream TV series via the web on my Ubuntu desktop, but I watch my movies on the DVD player.
- I use it because it has KDE 4 (which I simply love), and doubt Windows 7/8 or OSX will ever look and feel better (my better half recently asked me to remove all traces of Windows from her own computer and install KDE :D).

### Post by kagashe on 2012-06-18
> **caffeinatedev said:**
> Wow, that's fantastic to see an older person using Linux. Usually old people don't like changing their experience, especially when it comes to computing, if you don't mind my saying so ;)

I am 60 years old and I mind it very much. Prior to arrival of Windows 3.1 in the year 1992 I was 40 years old and had used computers running DOS and UNIX. For Data we were using dBase III which was developed on DOS but ported to UNIX also and Lotus 1-2-3. There was some programming knowledge required to use computers. I has learned Basic and used it for iterative type calculations.

Everything changed after arrival of Windows and MS Office but the generation which used dBase III never stopped learning. I knew about Linux but it became usable only when Xorg arrived to compete with Windows.I started using Mandrake Linux 9.1 in Sep 2004, switched to Ubuntu 5.04 in 2005 and never looked back.

Even in Windows 98 days I was using Star Office and other open source software.

In fact it is young generation which is learning MS Software and not willing to change.

Kamalakar

### Post by old_dog on 2012-06-19
Age 70 next birthday.....
Anything and everything. 
I must reply to the guy who seemed shocked and surprised that someone of 60(a mere stripling) was using linux. I started in computers in the mid 1960's on the main frames of the day, IBM of course there wasn't anything else. We had a big system 64K yes K. The only programming language available was "Assembler" It would take you ages to get a program working... er or was that just me! 
Remember the first screen...A major achievement to get it to say "Enter Part Number ........." and then do something with it.
This is going to sound a bit knobby but "been there done that" well most of the major innovations that are now mainstream.
Happily retired now just playing with any new toys.
Cheers 
old_dog

### Post by piperbarb on 2012-06-19
HaHa!  I had a good chuckle after reading caffeinatedev's comment about being so surprised that an "older person" uses Ubuntu.  :)  I'm 59, female, and have been using computers since I was a senior in high school (way, way back in the dark ages of the last century). 

I use Ubuntu for just about everything, except at work, where I have to use Windows.  Besides the obvious uses of e-mail, word processing/spreadsheets (using Libre Office), and web-surfing, I use Ubuntu for audio, video and image editing, and programming.  I love it, and will never go back to Windows.  

I do have 2 macbooks, one has only OS X (10.7.4), and the other is a dual boot, OS X (10.7.4)/Ubuntu 12.04.  I use the Ubuntu partition most of the time.  I also have two Dell laptops that are Ubuntu only machines.  I love the stability and speed.  There is nothing that I do that cannot be done in Ubuntu.

Even my Luddite spousal unit has been able to use Ubuntu without any difficulty or help from me.  Someday, when his fossil desktop Dell running WinXP finally kicks the bucket, his next computer will be running Ubuntu.

### Post by Autofac on 2013-01-28
I'm 25.

I moved out for good 3 years ago, at the end of my first degree, the place I was moving too wasn't big enough to have a desk that could handle monitor/desktop rig+laptop+lamp+reading space &c. I had to leave my PC at home and just make due with a laptop. I still visit my folks pretty frequently and when I was home I would play games and fiddle around with the computer.

About a year ago something caused a software crash which I could not recover from, try as I might I couldn't access my files--until I booted Ubuntu and was able to copy what I wanted onto a harddrive. I had lost my Windows XP disk so I resolved to install Ubuntu. I am only home once a week at the very most, but even less than that usually so it's a slow and steady process-figuring out Ubuntu, but I am enjoying it.

I did pop in a second drive and installed Windows only for Gaming and then just boot into Ubuntu for the rest. I am sure there are ways to make the games work in ubuntu, but the time I have at home I don't want to spend trying to figure that out, this is the easiest and best way for me.

Now is just a lot of tinkering to get the environment set up the way I want it. Currently it's 12.10 and 'Gnome Classic', though I do like Cinnamon as well.

### Post by llanitedave on 2013-03-13
The longer I avoid this thread the older I get!

57, and I use *Kubuntu* (hope that counts) for just about every thing I can.

The basics:
email, web surfing, music, watching movies, you tube

Development:
Python apps, piddly web pages, a bit of javascript

Work:
Development again, built a wxPython app for field use -- even though it'll be used on Windows, it was just easier to do most of the development using my linux tools.
Creating graphics and reports -- I have to put them on Windows eventually, but I hold off on that as long as I can.  I HATE the "ribbon interface".

Home away from home:
I work 300 miles from home and spent most of my nights in motel rooms.  I'd truly go crazy without my Linux laptop.

At home:
My wife runs Unity on her desktop, and I run Xubuntu on mine. We have side-by-side computing stations, where she works on her projects and I work on mine.  She's an artist, and I'm building a studio for her.  I did all the plans using Libre Office Draw.  Nice and simple, and does what I tell it to.  Unless I tell it to pick up a hammer and a saw...

### Post by meteorrock on 2013-03-13
14 years old here. And female. Using hypervisors and ubuntu to maintain the servers here at our school with the help of my computer teacher. Computers teachers "pet". lol. I use ubuntu for everything, gaming, surfing the web, the only thing that ties me to windows 7 at the moment is the lack of native games for linux and our ubuntu builds. Both OSes need more work in debugging and development IMHO.

### Post by RoosterHam on 2013-03-19
Age: 28

Use Ubuntu for: Everything. @ Work I use Ubuntu for research and study including surfing the web and online training, emails, testing OSes and software via LXC and KVM hosts (usually ubuntu or debian), for network design with Dia and writing configs and scripts for Linux servers and cisco/juniper network equipment.
@ Home Ubuntu has my desktop and 2 servers. Anything that a windows user does, I do it on my Ubuntu desktop. I don't have enough time for games. If I did, I'd use Ubuntu. The desktop also handles my hobby of tuning into ham radio bands and watching the psk31 and rtty traffic with fldigi, and I use JackMixer to clean up some voice signals (that's just my odd-ball idea that seems to be helping with some weak signal reception). One Ubuntu server is a file server (via samba for the housemates and via sshfs for myself) where I keep all the households movies, TV shows and music safe, along with providing a backup location to the household. The other Ubuntu server is my virtualisation host, hosting lxc/ubuntu containers that provide dns, dhcp, nagios and syslog. The vhost also has a Windows 2012 test system that I am comparing to Samba4.

### Post by Bill Tetzeli on 2013-03-23
Everything.  It used to be almost everything, except for certain music editing.  I make 24 bit/96 khz recordings at concerts.  In order for them to sound their best when converted to CD quality for musicians or friends who request copies, I resample them in r8brain Pro, dither them in Izotope Ozone 3 and resample them to 16 bit in a Samplitude or Audacity. My one big problem was making Ozone work in Linux.  It's not actually a program but a series of audio mastering plugins that have to be hosted in a WAV editing program.  The Linux version of Audacity wouldn't access Ozone installed under Wine, and Samplitude had its own problems.  Finally I hit on the idea of installing the Windows version of Audacity, which had no troubles accessing Ozone (other than the fact that the authorization screen kept sending me back to the same website because it wasn't sending the necessary authorization info - but that was solved simply by closing the authorization box and VOILA up pops the Ozone screen, at my beck and call :-) ).

So the good news is the last thread tying me to Windows has been snipped.  I don' need it fer nuthin' save helping out friends who have their own Windows problems.  Beyond the basics of browsing, email, social media, I use it for all types of video, as a music server playing the FLACs on my RAID over my stereo (which I access using x11vnc and Remmina), gaming (Open Arena, esp.) putting together my biweekly radio show and all my music editing needs.  The bad news is most of the music conversion is being done with Windows programs running in Wine - Audacity, Ozone, r8brain Pro, Trader's Little Helper, Cool Edit Pro - because the Linux alternatives don't have the same reliability or features, or they simply don't exist (the native version of Audacity suffices for most pre-conversion editing).  I'm grateful for the unpaid efforts of the Linux multimedia community, but the test of how good a program is is whether the pros are using it.  The pros are using Cakewalk, Samplitude, Wavelab, Cubase, Protools - they don't want to hear about Ardour, where you can't even do something as basic as zoom to the sample level, or Audacity, which won't tell you the exact volume level of different points in a noise envelope.  Like I said, I'm grateful for the work done, but I think the developers have to stop pretending it's bleeding edge when it's obvious on its face that it's not.

That said - to be free of the mindless vicissitudes of Microsoft, knowing I will never again get Ballmered by Redmond, trumps all.  Free at last! :-D

Oh right - 48, and get off my lawn! :P

### Post by ka55o5 on 2013-04-09
> **David Ostrom said:**
> **Voters: 1587**. This poll is closed

No vote 4 me..:(

Just turned 36, using (X)ubuntu for ***everything*** @work and @home; Windows is left on dual-boot for a few things, still, like:

[list][*]Dreamweaver
[*]Photoshop (Adobe Suite), no replacement for it
[*]Micro$oft Visio and MSO 2010 && 2013 (no replacement for it yet, unfortunately)
[*]Homegroup for sharing network printers and such (still a bit difficult 2 configure in *nix)
[*]Games, ofc.[/list]
PS.
I'm running the Oracle VM VirtualBox, with Windows XP 64-Bit Service Pack 2, but it's very limited for what I need Windows for and also having only 4GB of RAM doesn't help much. :f

PPS.
Oracle VM is set to use VDI and it's zer0-ed && compacted, something like this:
```
expertester.wordpress.com/2011/08/05/how-to-compact-virtualbox-vdi-virtual-disk-image-file/
```

**Edit**: Teh Ubuntu installer is (very much) awesome, btw. Never had to partition the HDD, just freed some space at the end of the partition && used the installer option to install alongside of Windows! =)

(and reinstalling goes like 1) booting from the Windows CD, or USB, 2) selecting repair and running "bootrec /fixboot" and "bootrec /fixmbr" 3) booting to windows and removing the linux partitions and 4) running the (X)ubuntu install again :))

### Post by Rob Sayer on 2013-07-14

### Post by wordpressuser on 2013-08-09
I am 62 and I use Ubuntu Server 10.04.02 LTS for home networking,a LAMP server and WordPress site. I use a different distro as a desktop, plus a separate windoze computer loaded with Adobe products. Recently, I started playing with a Raspberry Pi, making fair progress for a newbie so old I have forget something in order to learn something new. Getting brave, and impatient at the time it takes to write to the Pi's SD Card, I resurrected an older, though still decent, computer and loaded Ubuntu Server.

Using CLI's takes me back to DOS-5 and is a learning curve only slightly less frustrating than golf, but the tiniest success makes worthwhile the previous hour of hair pulling and gnashing of teeth. I have joined this forum in the hopes of receiving advice from people who, at some point, were as dense as I feel today!!

### Post by RobertKH on 2013-08-14
I'm 29 and use Ubuntu for running my NAS. I found its easy to use and FREE! Forget paying for windows services that are way to expensive and insecure. I haven't really been in the Ubuntu game for long. I used it as a kid, but never for anything other than installing packages and messing around with terminal. I tried a couple different forms of Ubuntu prior to settling on Ubuntu 12.10. It just felt a little cleaner. I also didn't want to have a terminal only application should my wife ever have to navigate it. Currently I run minidlna for streaming media to my TV and a FTP server for access via our iPhones. It seems to work really well. I also run mdadm raid setup for 2x1tb drives for family photos and videos and 2x320gb drives for iTunes media. We share our iTunes database through a shared thumb drive in our wireless modem. That works great for having a single iTunes library across multiple computers.

### Post by AnnaMary on 2013-09-03
I'm 39, and I use Ubuntu for most everything.  I recently got a "new to me pc" that had vista on it.  I couldn't deal with the bugginess of vista, so I ordered some new Linux distros. I have 8 live cd's on the way, but I'm sure I'll return to Ubuntu or Mint as usual.  :-)  Oh, I also have used Ubuntu to set up computers for older family members and friends who are just learning how to email, etc.  It has been a joy to bless them with a working computer without spending an arm and a leg to do so!

### Post by welshmike on 2013-09-06
I'm 71 and run dual boot Ubuntu 12.04 Unity and Windows XP. I also run W7, under Virtualbox. 
The Windows systems are there for me to run on occasion to investigate problems that neighbours have with their XP and W7 systems, especially malware and occasionally hardware, and clean up and fix their systems at no charge. 
My main use of this laptop is to run Ubuntu 12.04 Unity that initially I did not like after being used to 10.04 Gnome. 
However I am now very happy with 12.04 Unity. 
Ununtu has always been a stable and consistent fast performing system.
It is essential for me to run and keep track of my financial situation and also my lifestyle. I would be completely lost without it.
I started using Ubuntu in 2008 with 8.04 Hardy Heron after trying out many Linux distros, including Mint, Fedora, OpenSUSE, DSL and Puppy.
I found that I could make the appearance of Ubuntu 8.04 desktop and my use of Ubuntu similar to XP initially that made my transition from XP easier.
I've stuck with Ubuntu since 2008. especially because of the helpful community and Forum.
My background is 28 years in mainframe Systems Software development and I started using PCs at work as test tools for mainstream operating systems. So I have grown up through the use of DOS, Windows 3, 95, 98, NT, XP and OS/2.
My wife ran XP briefly on her laptop and when I installed Ubuntu on it she has never gone back to want to use XP. She now runs 10.04 Gnome which of course went end of life in May this year. I'm considering installing 12.04 Unity as well on her laptop.

### Post by ssrr84 on 2013-09-26
i'm 29 and i like playing around with new operation systems. i have installed Ubuntu alongside windows vista on my VAIO laptop. I'm very pleased that i was able to make all the hardwares work in Ubuntu (it is note worthy that even with Sony drivers from the product support page, i could not make all things working, every time some functionality is lost such as Bluetooth, webcam and mostly Fn keys). I do usual stuff in Ubuntu, surf the web, install and uninstall applications from different sources and ways (software center, terminal , downloading .db packages and ...).  i also watch movies (and i'm very pleased with the  default player which plays almost any thing). and i managed to install exFat and MTP support (i know, its a simple task to do for you guys, but i'm a newbie and doing such things is so exciting for me!!!!) so i could use my external 2TB hard drive (which is contains an extensive movie, TV show and documentary archive !!!!;)) and also my Galaxy S3 works with ubuntu fine. so i do not feel any urges to go to my windows environment unless i have to work with Photoshop ( to be honest, I do not do pro works with it so i think i can find a good alternative for it in Linux)

### Post by stevecook on 2013-12-05
50 years old. Been using it for over a year now. Kept windows as a second os in the beginning. Then simply had it as a VM on virtual box. Never use it for anything now.

I now have an Ubuntu LTSP network running at home. A main server in the study and two fat clients running off it. One in the living room and one in the bedroom. These are used for everything from office work to online tv/multimedia and also for web browsing. My reason for running an LTSP network instead of a simple peer to peer is cost savings. The two machines running as fat clients are laughably old and underpowered. But, all I had to do was add  bit of RAM and they run fine. Also, it means if I update the network apps, it's all done on the one machine (server) and so is nice and simple.

I think it may be obvious to one and all from what I have written above that I consider Ubuntu to be the dog's bollocks and will NEVER EVER go back to MS windows.

Except, of course, for Unity cos that sucks **really BAD**....:lolflag:

No probs, though. I just installed gnome shell over the top and it's all good. Especially after I found a nautilus patch that reinstalls all of the up/down/refresh arrows
